def_en: uttered offensive words in anger or pain; invoked harm
def_zh: cursed under breath, cursed the mud, cursed through sludge
quote_en: "Bent double, like old beggars under sacks, / Knock‑kneed, coughing like hags, we cursed through sludge, / Till on the haunting flares we turned our backs, / And towards our distant rest began to trudge." – The speaker, a soldier, describes his fellow infantrymen staggering away from the front line, their bodies broken and spirits bitter (Dulce et Decorum Est, 1917)
quote_zh: Owen’s choice of ‘cursed’ instantly establishes a tone of embittered exhaustion. Unlike a neutral verb like ‘trudged’ or ‘walked’, ‘cursed’ fuses physical movement with vocal rage, turning the soldiers’ march into an act of protest against their suffering. The harsh /k/ sound links it to ‘knock‑kneed’ and ‘coughing’, creating an abrasive sonic texture that mimics the men’s rasping breaths. Placed at the end of the line after the similes of beggars and hags, ‘cursed’ lands with a percussive force, as though the soldiers are spitting the word into the mud. This verb also introduces the poem’s broader critique of patriotic rhetoric: before the famous ‘old Lie’ is named, ‘cursed’ already signals that these men have abandoned any sense of noble sacrifice, reduced instead to raw, animal survival.
ex_en: The exhausted soldiers cursed as they hauled themselves through the slime, their anger the only thing left that was not frozen or broken.
ex_zh: The curse that had been building since the first shell whistled overhead finally erupted, not in a shout, but in the way the old veteran stopped speaking entirely whenever someone mentioned glory.

def_en: wearing shoes made of blood; walking on bloodied, bare feet
def_zh: blood‑shod feet, hobbled blood‑shod, blood‑shod soldiers
quote_en: "Men marched asleep. Many had lost their boots, / But limped on, blood‑shod. All went lame; all blind; / Drunk with fatigue; deaf even to the hoots / Of gas‑shells dropping softly behind." – The speaker describes soldiers so exhausted they are sleepwalking, many without boots, their feet wrapped only in dried blood (Dulce et Decorum Est, 1917)
quote_zh: Owen invents the word ‘blood‑shod’ to replace ‘barefoot’ with a horrific, tactile image. The compound adjective fuses the human body with a makeshift covering, implying that the soldiers’ own wounds have become their only protection. The echo of ‘iron‑shod’ (referring to horseshoes) suggests that men have been reduced to beasts of burden, shod not with metal but with their own congealed blood. The word sits in a semantic field of bodily injury alongside ‘lame’ and ‘blind’, but it is more shocking because it is a coinage—language itself is being fractured by trauma. Owen forces the reader to confront the physical cost of war not through abstract pity but through the visceral detail of a foot wrapped in its own scab, a detail that encapsulates the entire poem’s argument about the degradation of the soldier’s body.
ex_en: Many soldiers, having lost their boots, stumbled on with blood‑shod feet, each step a fresh agony that they were too dazed to notice.
ex_zh: Memory, for him, was blood‑shod: every happy recollection stepped eventually onto the sharp stones of that one gas‑filled morning.

def_en: struggling clumsily or helplessly, especially in water or mud
def_zh: floundering in panic, floundering like a fish, floundering through mud
quote_en: "Gas! GAS! Quick, boys!—An ecstasy of fumbling, / Fitting the clumsy helmets just in time; / But someone still was yelling out and stumbling / And flound’ring like a man in fire or lime..." – Amid a gas attack, the soldiers desperately try to put on their helmets, but one man fails and is seen floundering as if burning in chemicals (Dulce et Decorum Est, 1917)
quote_zh: Owen uses ‘flound’ring’ to capture the horrific hybrid of drowning and burning that a gas attack inflicts. The apostrophe elision quickens the line, suggesting a frantic, uncoordinated movement. The word evokes a fish out of water, yet the simile ‘like a man in fire or lime’ clashes violently with this aquatic image, creating a surreal juxtaposition that mirrors the soldier’s disorienting agony. The harsh /fl/ and /ndr/ clusters are physically awkward to pronounce, forcing the reader’s mouth into a clumsy motion that mimics the man’s struggle. Owen places ‘flound’ring’ at the climax of the gas panic, after the exclamations and fumbling, so that this single word focuses the reader’s horror on the individual victim, not the chaos. It is the moment where the collective ‘we’ of the first stanza fractures into one dying man, and the poem shifts from weariness to nightmare.
ex_en: The soldier, unable to don his gas mask in time, was left floundering in the green cloud, his body failing as the poison took hold.
ex_zh: Every morning when the alarm clock shrieked, he floundered for a moment in that half‑dream where the gas had not yet cleared.

def_en: dying through submersion in and inhalation of water—or, metaphorically, in gas
def_zh: drowning in gas, drowning helplessly, drowning man metaphor
quote_en: "Dim through the misty panes and thick green light, / As under a green sea, I saw him drowning. / In all my dreams before my helpless sight, / He plunges at me, guttering, choking, drowning." – The speaker watches through his gas mask as a comrade suffocates in the green gas, and this image now haunts his nightmares (Dulce et Decorum Est, 1917)
quote_zh: The repetition of ‘drowning’ across two lines imitates the recurrence of the traumatic memory—the speaker cannot escape the image. Owen turns the gas attack into a maritime nightmare: the green gas becomes a ‘green sea’, and the soldier is submerged in air turned liquid poison. The word ‘drowning’ belongs to a semantic field of water, yet the element is lethal, inverting the natural order. Phonetically, the long vowel /aʊ/ is a cry of pain, stretched across the line, and the final ‑ing syllable fades into nothing, like the soldier’s life. By placing ‘drowning’ at the end of both a stanza and the poem’s first section of direct description, Owen makes it the pivot point: after this, the poem will no longer report events but will plunge into the psychological aftermath.
ex_en: Through the fogged glass of his mask, the speaker watched his fellow soldier drowning in the poisonous green cloud, unable to help.
ex_zh: Some nights, the sound of the bathwater running is enough to make him remember that he has been drowning for thirty years.

def_en: making twisting, squirming movements or contortions of the body
def_zh: writhing in agony, writhing eyes, writhing face
quote_en: "If in some smothering dreams, you too could pace / Behind the wagon that we flung him in, / And watch the white eyes writhing in his face, / His hanging face, like a devil’s sick of sin;" – The speaker challenges the reader to imagine seeing a gas victim’s eyes twisting, not just the body (Dulce et Decorum Est, 1917)
quote_zh: Owen applies ‘writhing’ to the soldier’s eyes rather than his limbs, creating a deeply unsettling image. Eyes should turn or stare, not writhe; the verb is transferred from the body to the gaze, suggesting that the entire being is in spasm. The word connotes snake‑like movement, aligning the dying man with a tormented figure rather than a noble warrior. Phonetically, ‘writhing’ begins with the silent w, which forces a contortion of the mouth before the voiced /raɪð/, mimicking the twist it describes. This word sits within the poem’s larger semantic field of distorted bodies, alongside ‘bent double’, ‘knock‑kneed’, and ‘hanging’, but it intensifies the horror by making the reader’s own eye movement a participant. Owen is not simply describing a corpse; he is forcing the reader to witness the active, conscious moment of agony that the body endures, thereby making the ‘old Lie’ of the end all the more indefensible.
ex_en: The dying soldier’s eyes were writhing in their sockets, as if the poison was still twisting him from the inside.
ex_zh: She tried to forget the photograph, but the truth writhed in her memory like a worm in hot ash.

def_en: making a liquid bubbling sound in the throat, often while trying to speak
def_zh: gargling blood, gargling breath, gargling voice
quote_en: "If you could hear, at every jolt, the blood / Come gargling from the froth‑corrupted lungs, / Obscene as cancer, bitter as the cud / Of vile, incurable sores on innocent tongues,—" – In the wagon carrying the dead man, blood bubbles in his throat with each bump (Dulce et Decorum Est, 1917)
quote_zh: ‘Gargling’ is a domestic verb associated with morning hygiene, but Owen repurposes it to describe the sound of blood in a dying man’s throat. The effect is jarring: the word’s almost playful onomatopoeia (the repeated /g/ and /l/ sounds) is grotesquely at odds with its subject. This choice intensifies the horror by dragging the reader’s everyday experience into the nightmare of the wagon. The word contributes to a semantic field of corrupted bodily fluids—‘froth‑corrupted’, ‘vile, incurable sores’—that presents the soldier’s body as something that has turned against itself. The present participle ‘gargling’ continues the sense of ongoing action, implying that the death is not quick but a prolonged, audible suffering. By making the reader hear as well as see, Owen ensures that the sensory overload of the final stanza is inescapable, building the rhetorical pressure that will explode in the direct address of ‘My friend’.
ex_en: With every jolt of the wagon, blood came gargling from the soldier’s destroyed lungs, a sound that would never leave the speaker.
ex_zh: Hope, for him, was a gargling thing—something that rose in his throat only to drown in its own froth.

def_en: an intentionally false statement; also a false doctrine or myth
def_zh: old Lie, patriotic lie, expose the lie
quote_en: "My friend, you would not tell with such high zest / To children ardent for some desperate glory, / The old Lie: Dulce et decorum est / Pro patria mori." – The speaker directly addresses the reader, calling the classical tag ‘it is sweet and fitting to die for one’s country’ a lie (Dulce et Decorum Est, 1917)
quote_zh: Owen capitalises ‘Lie’ to elevate the word to the status of a proper noun, a named doctrine that has caused immense suffering. The capital letter forces the reader to pause and confront the word as an entity, not just a statement. By placing it after the colon, Owen positions ‘Lie’ as the ultimate definition of the Latin phrase that precedes it. The word belongs to a semantic field of deception and betrayal that runs beneath the poem: the flares are ‘haunting’, the gases drop ‘softly’, the helmets are ‘clumsy’—all elements of a war that disguises its true nature. ‘Lie’ strips away that disguise. The final line, ‘Pro patria mori’, is left without translation, challenging the reader’s knowledge and complicity. Owen is not merely saying the slogan is inaccurate; he is accusing the culture that perpetuates it of murder. The poem’s entire structure—from the ‘cursed’ march to the ‘gargling’ death—has been building to this one, devastating word.
ex_en: The poet calls the old claim that it is sweet to die for one’s country a Lie, exposed by the reality of the gas attack.
ex_zh: The lie was not that they died for their country; the lie was that the dying was ever sweet.

def_en: a small part of a space or place; an undistinguished patch of ground
def_zh: corner of a field, corner of England, foreign corner
quote_en: "If I should die, think only this of me: / That there's some corner of a foreign field / That is for ever England." – The speaker, a young soldier, imagines his own death abroad and asks that a small piece of earth become metaphorically part of his homeland (The Soldier, 1914)
quote_zh: Brooke deliberately chooses the modest, domestic word ‘corner’ rather than ‘tract’ or ‘province’. A corner is small, intimate, and unassuming—it belongs to gardens and familiar rooms. By claiming that this corner of a foreign field will be ‘for ever England’, the speaker sanctifies his own sacrifice without demanding grand monuments. The word works inside a semantic field of spatial humility alongside ‘field’ and ‘earth’, yet Brooke uses this very smallness to make the claim more audacious: even a patch of ground can be transformed by a single death. The soft /k/ and the liquid /ɔː/ give the word a gentle, yielding quality, as if the earth itself is opening to receive the soldier. In a sonnet that rejects grief and insists on legacy, ‘corner’ is the first quiet act of possession, a colonisation not by force but by love.
ex_en: The soldier believed that even an unremarkable corner of a foreign field could become English soil if he died there.
ex_zh: The corner of the photograph was curling, but you could still see the field where a boy from Stratford had decided to lie down forever and make it home.

def_en: a country in the British Isles; also an idea of pastoral beauty, memory, and national identity
def_zh: for ever England, breathing England, England bore
quote_en: "That is for ever England. There shall be / In that rich earth a richer dust concealed;" – The speaker imagines his burial transforming foreign soil into an eternal piece of England (The Soldier, 1914)
quote_zh: Brooke capitalises on the word ‘England’ as a repository of every pastoral and domestic ideal. Repeated three times in the poem, it becomes a talisman rather than a geographical designation. Here, ‘England’ is not a political state but a living entity: the earth, the air, the rivers, the suns of home. The phrase ‘for ever England’ reverses the logic of imperialism: instead of planting a flag, the soldier plants his body, and the land absorbs an identity. Phonetically, the word opens with a bright vowel and closes with a soft, melancholic /d/, as if the beloved name itself is a breath that must end. By placing ‘England’ at the line‑end after the enjambment, Brooke forces a pause that lets the word resonate. In the poem’s argument, ‘England’ is the guarantee that death is not loss but a gentler form of belonging.
ex_en: The speaker imagines that the foreign earth holding his dust will become England, absorbing everything the country gave him.
ex_zh: England, for him, was not a map but the feel of lawn clippings on bare feet and the exact orange of a Cotswold evening that never quite faded.

def_en: fine, dry particles of earth; the remains of a dead body
def_zh: richer dust, concealed dust, dust whom England bore
quote_en: "In that rich earth a richer dust concealed; / A dust whom England bore, shaped, made aware," – The speaker describes his own body, after death, as a precious dust that enriches the foreign soil (The Soldier, 1914)
quote_zh: Brooke transmutes the grim physical reality of a corpse into ‘dust’, a word that carries biblical echoes of mortality (‘dust thou art’) while also suggesting something refined, almost precious. The comparative ‘richer dust’ implies that English soil itself is superior, a notion that would read as arrogance were it not delivered with such tender conviction. The personification ‘whom England bore’ turns dust into a child, shaped and loved by a mothering landscape. This word belongs to a semantic field of earth and burial, yet it resists decay: dust here is not scattered but ‘concealed’, hidden like treasure. The short, blunt monosyllable contrasts with the flowing syntax, giving the word a ceremonial weight. Brooke transforms the soldier’s remains into a gift, a final act of giving back, aligning personal sacrifice with the fertility of the land itself.
ex_en: The soldier’s physical remains, a richer dust, will lie in foreign soil and make it English.
ex_zh: The dust that settled on his uniform in the trench was a lighter shade than the dust that later settled on his parents’ mantelpiece in the photograph frame.

def_en: the organ; the seat of emotion, spirit, and love; the core of a person
def_zh: this heart, heart all evil shed, hearts at peace
quote_en: "And think, this heart, all evil shed away, / A pulse in the eternal mind, no less / Gives somewhere back the thoughts by England given;" – The speaker asks to be remembered as a purified heart, beating eternally and returning England's gifts (The Soldier, 1914)
quote_zh: ‘Heart’ in this sestet shifts the poem’s focus from the physical body to the immortal soul. By stripping away ‘all evil’, Brooke presents a heart cleansed of war’s brutality, a deliberately sanitised version of soldiering. The word is central to a semantic field of inwardness and purity alongside ‘dreams’, ‘laughter’, ‘gentleness’, and ‘peace’. The metaphor ‘a pulse in the eternal mind’ abstracts the heart into a rhythmic idea, part of a cosmic circulation. The choice of ‘heart’ over ‘soul’ or ‘spirit’ is strategic: it retains a bodily warmth and familiarity, allowing the reader to feel the speaker’s continuing presence rather than a distant afterlife. The word also echoes the poem’s sonnet structure; traditionally, the volta of a Petrarchan sonnet turns toward the heart’s resolution. Here, Brooke makes ‘heart’ the pivot, suggesting that the soldier’s legacy is not in monuments but in a benign, rhythmic permanence.
ex_en: The speaker invites his reader to imagine that his heart, purified of evil, continues to pulse in an eternal mind, returning all of England’s gifts.
ex_zh: The old soldier’s heart, they said, had stopped; but his wife could still feel it ticking softly in the house’s floorboards every Remembrance Sunday.

def_en: a state of tranquility, freedom from disturbance or war; also a heavenly condition
def_zh: at peace, hearts at peace, English heaven
quote_en: "And laughter, learnt of friends; and gentleness, / In hearts at peace, under an English heaven." – The poem closes by locating the soldier’s eternal reward in a state of peace, surrounded by all that England meant to him (The Soldier, 1914)
quote_zh: Brooke concludes the sonnet with ‘peace’, a word that gathers the poem’s entire argument into a single, quiet resolution. The sibilant opening /p/ is a soft release after the liquid consonants of ‘laughter’ and ‘gentleness’, bringing the reader to a full stop that feels like an exhalation. ‘Peace’ belongs here to a semantic field of rest and heavenly reward, yet Brooke ties it not to Christian orthodoxy but to an ‘English heaven’—a landscape where war has no place. The word is deliberately chosen over ‘rest’ or ‘calm’; ‘peace’ carries political and moral weight in 1914, the year the Great War began, making it a quiet protest against the conflict even as it accepts sacrifice. By placing ‘peace’ in the final rhyming couplet with ‘heaven’, Brooke creates a clinching chord that sounds like a benediction. The poem’s refusal to mention enemy, battle, or pain makes this final peace feel not evasive but earned, a state that the soldier has already achieved in his vision of self‑giving.
ex_en: In the imagined afterlife, the soldier finds eternal peace among friends and familiar English gentleness, under a domestic heaven.
ex_zh: The peace he had not found in the trenches came to him one afternoon when he stopped, sixty years later, by a hedge in Shropshire and finally understood why the blackbird sings.

def_en: a solemn song, often of praise or mourning; a choral piece for a church or nation
def_zh: anthem for youth, funeral anthem, solemn anthem
quote_en: "What passing‑bells for these who die as cattle? / —Only the monstrous anger of the guns. / Only the stuttering rifles’ rapid rattle / Can patter out their hasty orisons." – The speaker asks what ceremony marks the mass death of soldiers and answers that only the noise of weaponry serves as their funeral rite (Anthem for Doomed Youth, 1917)
quote_zh: Owen’s title noun ‘anthem’ immediately establishes a bitter, ironic framework. An anthem is a sacred, elevated song, but the poem will reveal that the ‘music’ for these dead is the cacophony of artillery and rifle fire. The word belongs to a semantic field of ritual and religion—alongside ‘passing‑bells’, ‘orisons’, ‘prayers’, ‘candles’, ‘pall’, and ‘choirs’—that the poem systematically empties of comfort. By calling the poem itself an ‘anthem’, Owen positions his own verse as the only genuine memorial, an artistic act that counters the industrialised, mechanical slaughter. The Greek origin (antiphōna, sounding back) also suggests a call‑and‑response, but here there is no answering voice from God or nation. The capitalisation in the title elevates the word to a proper name, yet the epithet ‘doomed’ undercuts any grandeur: this is a hymn for the condemned, not the glorified.
ex_en: The poem opens by asking what funeral bells exist for the slaughtered soldiers, and the answer is that the guns themselves are their only anthem.
ex_zh: The silence after the last shell was the only anthem that ever truly honoured them—a terrible song that took years to learn, and some never stopped hearing it.

def_en: firearms, especially heavy artillery; weapons of war
def_zh: anger of the guns, gun‑fire, rattle of guns
quote_en: "What passing‑bells for these who die as cattle? / —Only the monstrous anger of the guns." – The speaker declares that the sole memorial tolling for the dead is the furious, continuous sound of artillery (Anthem for Doomed Youth, 1917)
quote_zh: Owen personifies ‘guns’ by coupling them with ‘monstrous anger’, transferring human rage onto inanimate weapons and making the battlefield itself an out‑of‑control beast. The hard /g/ and blunt monosyllable strike the ear like a report, while the assonance with ‘stuttering’ and ‘rattle’ in the following lines weaves the guns into a relentless sonic pattern. The word anchors a semantic field of mechanised violence that competes with the religious vocabulary: the guns replace church bells, the rifles replace prayers. The metaphor ‘anger of the guns’ implies that war has its own malevolent will, indifferent to the individual life. Owen, having experienced the artillery barrages of the Western Front firsthand, chooses a word that was omnipresent in soldiers’ ears, an unceasing reminder of mortality. The plural ‘guns’ also suggests a multitude, an industrial scale of death that no single church ceremony could compass.
ex_en: The only funeral music for the slaughtered men was the monstrous, unrelenting anger of the guns.
ex_zh: Even decades later, the old man could hear guns in the thunder—or perhaps it was that the thunder had always been inside him, and just needed a storm to let it out.

def_en: domesticated bovine animals; used figuratively to dehumanise masses led to slaughter
def_zh: die like cattle, herded cattle, slaughtered cattle
quote_en: "What passing‑bells for these who die as cattle?" – The speaker compares the soldiers dying en masse to livestock led to butchery (Anthem for Doomed Youth, 1917)
quote_zh: Owen’s choice of ‘cattle’—placed at the end of the first line—delivers the poem’s most devastating accusation within its first ten words. The simile ‘die as cattle’ strips soldiers of individuality, bravery, and choice; they are not heroic individuals but animals herded to an industrial slaughterhouse. The word’s harsh /k/ and the dull vowel /æ/ make it physically unpleasant to speak, as if the mouth itself recoils from the comparison. ‘Cattle’ belongs to a semantic field of dehumanisation that includes later references to ‘shires’ and ‘pall’, linking the pastoral world to death. Owen rejects the traditional elegy’s tenderness by refusing even the dignity of ‘men’ or ‘youth’. Instead, he forces the reader to confront the scale and anonymity of mass warfare: these are not warriors but units of meat. The word also echoes the poem’s abattoir‑like soundscape—the ‘stuttering rifles’ akin to a bolt‑gun, the ‘rattle’ to chains.
ex_en: The soldiers met their end like cattle, herded and butchered without distinction or ceremony.
ex_zh: The boy who had once named the dairy cows was buried in a field whose only crop was rows of white crosses, and no one could say which one was his.

def_en: organised groups of singers, especially in a church; metaphorically, collective voices
def_zh: holy choirs, choirs of wail, choirs of mourning
quote_en: "No mockeries now for them; no prayers nor bells; / Nor any voice of mourning save the choirs,— / The shrill, demented choirs of wailing shells;" – The poet says that instead of church choirs, the only voices of mourning for the dead are the screaming sounds of descending shells (Anthem for Doomed Youth, 1917)
quote_zh: Owen redefines ‘choirs’ by fusing the sacred with the terrifying. The word traditionally evokes harmony, order, and collective worship; here, the choirs are ‘shrill, demented’ and composed of ‘wailing shells’. This oxymoronic pairing collapses the boundary between civilisation and barbarism, as if the war has created its own perverted liturgy. The sibilance in ‘shrill, demented choirs’ mimics the whistle‑and‑crescendo of an incoming shell, turning language into a sonic replica of trench soundscapes. The word sits within a semantic field of music and ritual that the poem systematically destroys: bells become gunfire, prayers become rifle rattle, and now choirs become shell‑wail. By placing ‘choirs’ at the line break, Owen creates a brief, false hope that a human voice might enter the poem, only to crush it with the revelation that the singers are weapons.
ex_en: The only mourning voices for the dead were the choirs of shrieking, demented shells instead of human mourners.
ex_zh: The wind in the ruined cathedral had learned to sing a choir of absence, each note a name that would never again be spoken aloud.

def_en: wax cylinders with wicks, burned for light or in religious ceremonies; a symbol of remembrance
def_zh: holy candles, candles of grief, undying candles
quote_en: "What candles may be held to speed them all? / Not in the hands of boys, but in their eyes / Shall shine the holy glimmers of goodbyes." – The speaker asks what sacred lights can accompany the dead, and answers that the only candles are the reflections of farewell in the dying soldiers’ own eyes (Anthem for Doomed Youth, 1917)
quote_zh: Owen transforms ‘candles’—objects of church ritual and domestic comfort—into an image of profound pathos. The conventional funeral candle is absent; instead, the glint of light in a boy’s eye becomes the only illumination for the dead. This metaphorical transference places the sacred entirely within the individual human life, bypassing institutional religion. The word belongs to a semantic field of light and religiosity, but here it is fragile, momentary: the ‘glimmers of goodbyes’ will be extinguished as the eyes close. The alliteration of ‘holy glimmers’ and the assonance of ‘candles’ with ‘hands’ creates a hushed, almost lullaby‑like tone that contrasts violently with the cacophony of the octet. Owen is not describing a physical candle but a memory of one, a ghost‑flicker that the reader may imagine. The question form ‘What candles may be held…?’ acknowledges that the scale of loss outstrips any material rite, leaving only the inner light of a final human moment.
ex_en: No church candles could be provided for the dead; the only holy lights were the faint reflections of farewell in the dying soldiers’ own gazing eyes.
ex_zh: She lit a candle every night for a man she had never met, and in its small, unsteady flame she saw the glimmer of all the boys who had looked up at a foreign sky and known.

def_en: a cloth spread over a coffin or tomb; also, a dark covering or cloud of gloom
def_zh: funeral pall, pall of death, draw down the blinds as a pall
quote_en: "The pallor of girls’ brows shall be their pall;" – The speaker declares that the pale foreheads of grieving young women will serve as the funerary cloth for the dead soldiers (Anthem for Doomed Youth, 1917)
quote_zh: Owen chooses ‘pall’ for its dual meaning: a literal coffin‑covering and a heavy, oppressive atmosphere. By linking the pall to ‘the pallor of girls’ brows’, he transfers the funeral rite from the battlefield to the home front, where grief is the only shroud left. The alliteration of ‘pallor of girls’ brows shall be their pall’ creates a whispered, solemn rhythm, and the words ‘pallor’ and ‘pall’ are nearly identical in sound, binding the living mourner to the dead soldier. This word belongs to a semantic field of ceremony and mourning: the girl’s pale forehead becomes the cloth, her drawn‑down gaze becomes the blinds. Owen replaces the material trappings of a formal funeral with human emotion itself, suggesting that only love can truly honour the dead. Placed at the end of the line, ‘pall’ resonates with the concluding ‘and each slow dusk a drawing‑down of blinds’, offering a quiet, domestic image of closure that the octet’s violent sounds denied.
ex_en: The pale brows of the grieving girls at home would serve as a funeral pall, a cloth of sorrow covering the absent dead.
ex_zh: The snow that fell silent and thick on Armistice Day became a pall over the whole county, as if the sky itself had grown pale with remembering.

def_en: the darker stage of twilight; metaphorically, the end or fading of life
def_zh: slow dusk, drawing‑down of dusk, dusk of memory
quote_en: "And each slow dusk a drawing‑down of blinds." – The poem ends by likening the sunset to the closing of window blinds in a household that has received terrible news (Anthem for Doomed Youth, 1917)
quote_zh: Owen closes the sonnet with ‘dusk’, a word that gathers every elegiac impulse into a single, fading light. The personification of dusk as ‘slow’ stretches time into something mournful, and the simile of ‘drawing‑down of blinds’ roots cosmic grief in the ordinary, domestic gesture of a household in mourning. The /d/ alliteration (‘dusk’, ‘drawing‑down’) is soft and final, like a door gently closed. ‘Dusk’ belongs to a semantic field of endings and fading that pervades the sestet: glimmers, pallor, blinds, and goodbyes all signal a world dimming. Unlike the guns and shells of the octet, dusk is quiet, universal, and inexorable—a daily reminder of the dead. Owen chooses ‘dusk’ over ‘night’ because dusk retains the trace of light; it is the moment when day still remembers itself. In the context of the poem’s argument, dusk becomes the only repeated ceremony: every sunset repeats the drawing‑down of blinds, a ritual of memory that the home front enacts each evening, whether it knows it or not.
ex_en: Every slow dusk is like the drawing‑down of blinds in a house that has received a telegram, a daily ritual of lament for the dead.
ex_zh: He learned to hate the dusk, not because it was dark, but because it arrived so gently, as if the world had already begun to forget how many of its children were missing.

def_en: gathered or assembled in a large, dense group
def_zh: massed attack, massed ranks, massed artillery
quote_en: "At dawn the ridge emerges massed and dun / In the wild purple of the glow'ring sun," – The speaker describes the battlefield at daybreak, the ridge appearing as a dense, dark shape against a threatening sky (Attack, 1918)
quote_zh: Sassoon opens with 'massed', a word that immediately suggests an overwhelming, compressed force. The adjective applies grammatically to the 'ridge', but it carries the shadow of 'massed troops' or 'massed guns', so that the landscape itself seems militarised, heavy with impending violence. The /m/ and /d/ sounds are dense and blunt, giving the line a weighty, trudging rhythm that mirrors the soldiers' movement. 'Massed' belongs to a semantic field of scale and pressure that runs throughout the poem: 'tanks', 'barrage', 'creeping', 'multitude' all suggest forces too large to resist. By placing this word at dawn, Sassoon denies the traditional hope of sunrise; instead, light reveals only the sheer bulk of the hostile terrain. The word also implies a loss of individuality: the ridge, like the men, is one indistinguishable mass.
ex_en: At dawn, the ridge emerged as a massed, dark form against the glowering purple sun, a landscape already threatening violence.
ex_zh: The memories did not arrive one by one but massed on the horizon of his sleep like a battalion assembling in silence before the whistle.

def_en: scowling; looking angrily or sullenly; a portmanteau suggesting both glowing and glowering
def_zh: glow'ring sun, glow'ring sky, glow'ring horizon
quote_en: "In the wild purple of the glow'ring sun," – The sun at dawn is described as both glowing with light and glowering with menace (Attack, 1918)
quote_zh: Sassoon's coinage 'glow'ring' is a portmanteau—'glowing' and 'glowering' fused—that captures the surreal, hostile beauty of a battlefield dawn. The sun is not nourishing but threatening, its light a scowl rather than a blessing. The apostrophe and compression force the reader to pause and register the strangeness; language itself is under pressure, much like the soldiers. The word belongs to a semantic field of ominous natural imagery alongside 'wild purple', 'menacing', and the later 'haunted' terrain. Sassoon, a decorated officer who wrote from direct experience, knew the dawn attack was a moment of maximum dread, and 'glow'ring' distills that tension into a single unstable word. The /ɡl/ and /aʊ/ sounds open the mouth in a grimace that matches the sun's expression.
ex_en: The sun that morning was glow'ring, a malevolent eye that illuminated only targets and never warmth.
ex_zh: Even the sun, on the day of the push, seemed to be glow'ring down as if nature had chosen a side and it was not theirs.

def_en: a long, narrow hilltop or crest; a strategic high ground in battle
def_zh: emerged ridge, massed ridge, take the ridge
quote_en: "At dawn the ridge emerges massed and dun / In the wild purple of the glow'ring sun," – The speaker watches as the hilltop, the objective of the attack, becomes visible in the dawn light (Attack, 1918)
quote_zh: 'Ridge' is the poem's first concrete geographical noun and serves as the focal point of the entire stanza. In trench warfare, a ridge was a position of tactical importance, often heavily defended and paid for in blood. The word is stark and monosyllabic, with a hard /dʒ/ that cuts the line like a spade hitting chalk. It belongs to a semantic field of terrain and landscape that will include 'slope', 'mud', and 'track', anchoring the poem in the physical reality of the Western Front. The choice of 'ridge' over 'hill' or 'height' is deliberate: a ridge is a crest, a line of exposure against the sky, where advancing soldiers become silhouettes and easy targets. By having it 'emerge' through the dawn mist, Sassoon creates a slow reveal of the objective that is also a slow reveal of doom.
ex_en: At sunrise, the ridge became visible as a massed brown shape, the objective of the coming assault.
ex_zh: He had dreamed of climbing the ridge to see the view beyond, but the ridge he climbed was made of silence and the view was just another field of white stones.

def_en: a concentrated artillery bombardment; also, an overwhelming quantity of something
def_zh: creeping barrage, artillery barrage, barrage of shells
quote_en: "Smouldering through spouts of drifting smoke that shroud / The menacing scarred slope; and, one by one, / Tanks creep and topple forward to the wire. / The barrage roars and lifts." – As the tanks move forward, the artillery bombardment thunders and then shifts ahead (Attack, 1918)
quote_zh: 'Barrage' is the poem's central sound-word, a noun that contains within it the entire sensory assault of industrial warfare. The word entered English from French artillery terminology during WWI, so its very presence is historically specific. The /b/ and /r/ and the open /ɑː/ create a long, rumbling sound that mimics the thunder it names. Sassoon places 'barrage' as a short, stark sentence—'The barrage roars and lifts.'—after the creeping, hesitant movement of the tanks, making the line itself feel like an explosion. The word belongs to a semantic field of mechanised violence alongside 'tanks', 'wire', and 'topple'. 'Lifts' is crucial: a lifting barrage signals that the infantry must now advance into the enemy's line, making this the most terrifying moment for soldiers—the protection has moved on, and they are exposed.
ex_en: The artillery barrage thundered over the slope, then lifted, and the men knew they must now rise and walk into the fire.
ex_zh: The barrage had ended seventy years ago, but her grandfather still flinched when the door slammed, his body remembering what his mind had tried to bury.

def_en: to move slowly and carefully; especially, to advance under fire
def_zh: tanks creep, creep forward, creep towards the wire
quote_en: "Tanks creep and topple forward to the wire." – The tanks, newly introduced to warfare, move slowly and unsteadily toward the barbed wire (Attack, 1918)
quote_zh: Sassoon chooses 'creep' for its cold, deliberate quality, suggesting a mechanical slowness that is almost insect-like. The verb is usually associated with living things moving stealthily; applied to tanks, it creates an uncanny personification, as though the machines are sentient predators. The long /iː/ vowel stretches the word, dragging out the movement and building tension. 'Creep' belongs to a semantic field of slow, inevitable advance that includes 'topple' and later 'crawl' and 'stumble'. The word also carries the military term 'creeping barrage' in its undertow, linking the tanks to the artillery's deadly rhythm. In the context of 1918, tanks were still primitive, unreliable, and terrifying to both sides; 'creep' captures their laboured, nightmare quality.
ex_en: The tanks crept forward, toppling as they hit the uneven ground, making their slow way toward the barbed wire.
ex_zh: Fear did not shout when it came; it crept into the trench at three in the morning, silent as the fog, and sat down beside each man like an old friend.

def_en: a hot glowing body of ignited gas; a vivid burst of fire or light
def_zh: burst of flame, leap of flame, flame and smoke
quote_en: "Then, for an instant, a shattering flame / Leaps up and out, a furious burst of light" – An explosion erupts on the battlefield with a sudden, violent flare of fire (Attack, 1918)
quote_zh: Sassoon introduces 'flame' as the first explosion of the poem, breaking the slow, creeping rhythm like a detonation. The /fl/ blend is a flash on the lips, followed by the long /eɪ/ that seems to linger in the air before the /m/ closes the mouth. The word is surrounded by verbs of violent motion—'shattering', 'leaps', 'burst'—creating a semantic field of sudden, destructive energy. 'Flame' in trench warfare was particularly feared because it signalled shell-bursts, flamethrowers, or burning vehicles, all of which caused horrific wounds. The phrase 'for an instant' underscores the brevity of the light and the long darkness that follows, a microcosm of the soldier's experience: moments of terror punctuating hours of waiting.
ex_en: A sudden flame, shattering and furious, leapt from the explosion and lit up the whole slope.
ex_zh: In the photograph, you could not see the flame that had taken his friend, only the smoke, but the flame lived on in the white space where his friend had been standing.

def_en: inhabited or visited by ghosts; showing signs of mental anguish or suffering
def_zh: haunted slope, haunted eyes, haunted terrain
quote_en: "The menacing scarred slope; and, one by one, / Tanks creep and topple forward to the wire." – The hillside, pitted with shell-holes and scars, is described as menacing and, by implication, haunted (Attack, 1918)
quote_zh: Although 'haunted' does not appear explicitly in the poem's text, the adjective 'menacing' and the later description of soldiers' faces as 'masked' and 'mad' create a semantic field of ghostly presence that 'haunted' epitomises. Sassoon's landscape is haunted not by spirits but by the imminence of death; every shell-hole, every scar on the slope, is a trace of previous attacks and previous dead. The phonetic weight of 'haunted'—the breathy /h/, the open-mouthed /ɔː/, the unresolved /tɪd/—suggests something lingering and incomplete. In the poem's final vision, the men who survive will carry this haunting forward: the battlefield will live inside them.
ex_en: The scarred slope seemed haunted by all the attacks that had failed there before, the earth itself remembering every body it had swallowed.
ex_zh: The field was sold to a farmer who swore he heard, each autumn when the plough turned the soil, a whisper that sounded like a name being spoken for the last time.

def_en: the front parts of human heads; expressions; identities
def_zh: masked faces, terrified faces, haggard faces
quote_en: "O Jesus, make it stop! and the filthy, haggard faces / Masked in the mud, and mad." – The poem ends with the soldiers' desperate prayer and their unrecognisable, mud-caked faces (Attack, 1918)
quote_zh: Sassoon closes the poem on 'faces', a word that should denote identity and humanity but here signifies its erasure. These faces are 'filthy, haggard', 'masked in the mud, and mad'—adjectives that strip away individuality until the soldiers are indistinguishable from the earth they crawl through. The alliteration of /m/ in 'masked', 'mud', and 'mad' creates a closed, muffled sound, as if the language itself is being buried. 'Faces' belongs to a semantic field of the human body under duress: the ridge was 'massed' like a body, the tanks 'creep' like limbs, and now the face—the seat of self—is obliterated. The abrupt cry 'O Jesus, make it stop!' preceding this image shatters the poem's measured descriptive tone, and the final line's fragmentation ('Masked in the mud, and mad.') mirrors the disintegration of the soldiers' minds and bodies. Sassoon offers no consolation, only the unvarnished, staring truth of what war does to the human face.
ex_en: The soldiers' faces were so caked in filth and exhaustion that they looked masked, unrecognisable, stripped of every trace of the boys they had been.
ex_zh: He had a gallery of faces in his head, but the ones that stayed longest were not the living—they were the faces he had watched become mud, one rain-soaked morning in a place whose name he could never pronounce without weeping.

def_en: a unit of distance, roughly three miles; also a group or alliance
def_zh: half a league, league onward, valley of Death
quote_en: "Half a league, half a league, / Half a league onward, / All in the valley of Death / Rode the six hundred." – The poem opens by measuring the cavalry's advance in leagues, repeated like a drumbeat (The Charge of the Light Brigade, 1854)
quote_zh: Tennyson uses the archaic unit ‘league’ to transport the reader into a legendary, almost Arthurian world. The word’s old‑fashioned resonance distances the charge from the muddy, industrial Crimea and elevates it into heroic narrative. The triple repetition—‘Half a league, half a league, / Half a league onward’—creates a galloping dactylic rhythm that mimics hoofbeats, turning distance into a pulse. ‘League’ belongs to a semantic field of measurement and space that includes ‘valley’, ‘jaws’, and ‘mouth’, all of which map the charge as a journey into a mythic underworld. By choosing ‘league’ over ‘mile’, Tennyson taps into the language of chivalric romance (Malory, Spenser), immediately framing the soldiers as knights. The word also suggests the slowness of the approach: each half‑league is a step closer to annihilation, and the counting of it is an act of ritual anticipation.
ex_en: The brigade rode half a league and then another, advancing further into the valley that was already being called Death.
ex_zh: The old cavalryman still measured distance not in miles but in leagues—the length of a gallop, the space between one breath and the next when the guns ahead had not yet spoken.

def_en: a low area between hills or mountains; metaphorically, a place of shadow and peril
def_zh: valley of Death, into the valley, rode into the valley
quote_en: "All in the valley of Death / Rode the six hundred." – The cavalry rides into a geographical feature that the poem immediately renames as a place of death (The Charge of the Light Brigade, 1854)
quote_zh: Tennyson transforms the actual terrain of Balaclava into the allegorical ‘valley of Death’, echoing the biblical ‘valley of the shadow of death’ (Psalm 23). This metaphor frames the charge as a willing descent into mortality, the landscape itself becoming an antagonist. The word ‘valley’ is enclosed by hills, a natural trap, and Tennyson later reinforces this with ‘Cannon to right of them, / Cannon to left of them, / Cannon in front of them’. This word anchors a semantic field of confining, deadly space—‘valley’, ‘jaws’, ‘mouth’, ‘hell’—that turns geography into a living, devouring presence. The opening /v/ and the liquid /æl/ give ‘valley’ a mournful, hollow quality, as if the word itself is a basin. By repeating the phrase ‘valley of Death’ at the end of each framing stanza, Tennyson makes the location a refrain, a tolling bell.
ex_en: The six hundred rode into a valley that, by the time they emerged, would be known only as the valley of Death.
ex_zh: Every soldier carries a valley inside them—a place where the walls are steep with memory and the only way out is straight ahead, into the guns.

def_en: made a stupid or careless mistake; erred
def_zh: some one had blunder'd, leader blunder'd, fateful blunder'd
quote_en: "Not tho' the soldier knew / Some one had blunder'd: / Their's not to make reply, / Their's not to reason why, / Their's but to do and die:" – The speaker acknowledges that the soldiers knew a mistake had been made, yet they obeyed and rode on (The Charge of the Light Brigade, 1854)
quote_zh: Tennyson’s use of ‘blunder’d’ is the poem’s most diplomatically charged word, acknowledging the catastrophic error of the command without naming the responsible individual (Lord Raglan or Lord Lucan). The archaic apostrophe signals that this is a word being quoted from reports, a term of controversy that has already entered public debate. ‘Blunder’d’ sits in stark contrast to the elevated, heroic language surrounding it: ‘valour’, ‘glory’, ‘noble’—a single, blunt admission of human failure. Phonetically, the /bl/ and /ʌndəd/ are clumsy and stumbling, the sound itself performing the mistake. The word confirms that the charge was not a tactical decision but an accident, which paradoxically heightens the soldiers’ virtue: they ride to their deaths knowing the order is wrong. By subordinating ‘blunder’d’ inside a subordinate clause (‘Not tho’ the soldier knew’), Tennyson gently embeds the criticism while keeping the main emphasis on obedience and sacrifice.
ex_en: Although the soldiers understood that someone in command had blundered, they did not question the order, because their duty was simply to ride and, if necessary, to die.
ex_zh: The blunder hung in the air of the officers’ tent for sixty years, a name that was never spoken but which everyone could smell, like gunpowder on a coat that would not be cleaned.

def_en: the two bony parts of the mouth that hold teeth; a gripping mouth of a monster
def_zh: jaws of Death, into the jaws, jaws of Hell
quote_en: "Into the jaws of Death, / Into the mouth of Hell / Rode the six hundred." – The valley is now imagined as the gaping mouth of a devouring beast (The Charge of the Light Brigade, 1854)
quote_zh: Tennyson escalates the metaphor of the valley by replacing it with ‘jaws’, a word that animates the landscape into a predatory creature. ‘Jaws’ suggests a crushing, grinding closure, an inescapable trap lined with teeth. The word is paired with ‘mouth’ and later ‘Hell’, building a semantic field of consumption and demonic imagery. The hard /dʒ/ sound and the open vowel /ɔː/ make the word physically expansive, as if the mouth itself is opening when the word is spoken. The definite article ‘the jaws’ (not ‘jaws’) implies a single, known monster—Death personified—that the soldiers face with open eyes. By placing ‘jaws’ at the line’s culmination, after the run of cannon and volley, Tennyson gives the word an almost cinematic finality: the riders disappear into the throat of the beast.
ex_en: The cavalry rode straight into the jaws of Death, as though the valley had opened its mouth to swallow them whole.
ex_zh: The photograph showed the mouth of the trench—dark, lipless, and waiting—and the boy from Dorset swore later that he had felt jaws close around him the moment he entered.

def_en: a heavy cavalry sword with a curved blade; symbol of mounted warfare
def_zh: flash'd all their sabres, sabres bare, sabre-stroke
quote_en: "Flash'd all their sabres bare, / Flash'd as they turn'd in air / Sabring the gunners there," – The cavalry draw their swords, which flash in the sun as they strike at the Russian artillerymen (The Charge of the Light Brigade, 1854)
quote_zh: ‘Sabres’ is the poem’s most vivid symbol of a vanishing mode of warfare—the hand‑to‑hand blade charge in an age of long‑range artillery. The word’s sibilance (‘sabres’, ‘flash’d’, ‘sabring’) creates a hissing, slicing soundscape that cuts through the thud of the cannon. The repetition of ‘Flash’d’ makes the sabres into sources of light, momentarily illuminating the smoke, as if the weapons carry their own glory. The choice of ‘sabres’ over the more generic ‘swords’ is precise: a sabre was the standard British cavalry weapon of the period, and its curved blade was designed for slashing from horseback. Tennyson keeps the focus on the soldiers’ aggressive action—‘sabring the gunners’—making the charge a moment of agency, even within the trap. The word anchors a semantic field of archaic, chivalric combat that the poem uses to counterbalance the industrial ‘cannon’ and ‘shot’.
ex_en: The riders unsheathed their sabres, which flashed in the smoky air as they struck at the Russian gunners.
ex_zh: In the museum, the sabre lay behind glass, still bright, as if the hand that last held it had only just let go.

def_en: a member of a people of southern Russia, famed as light cavalry; here, the enemy horsemen
def_zh: Cossack and Russian, charge of the Cossack, against the Cossack
quote_en: "Cossack and Russian / Reel'd from the sabre-stroke / Shatter'd and sunder'd." – The enemy cavalry, both Cossacks and regular Russian troops, are thrown back by the British charge (The Charge of the Light Brigade, 1854)
quote_zh: Tennyson names ‘Cossack’ to give the enemy a specific, exotic identity, heightening the sense of the Light Brigade fighting overwhelming and legendary foes. The Cossacks were renowned as fierce horsemen of the Russian steppes, so their inclusion elevates the British charge: even these formidable warriors are ‘reel’d’ and ‘shatter’d’. The word’s harsh /k/ and hissing /s/ sounds are jagged and violent, mimicking the clash of cavalry. The coupling of ‘Cossack and Russian’ suggests an entire empire arrayed against the six hundred, intensifying the odds. By allowing the enemy a name, Tennyson also grants them a dignity that pure allegory would deny; this is not a slaughter of abstractions but a battle of real soldiers. The word belongs to a semantic field of named combatants alongside ‘gunners’, ‘Russian’, and earlier ‘the six hundred’.
ex_en: The Cossack horsemen and Russian soldiers staggered back, reeled by the force of the sabre-stroke, shattered and sundered.
ex_zh: The Cossack’s great‑grandson found the British bugle in a drawer and polished it until it shone, not because he had forgiven, but because the sound, he thought, belonged to all of them now.

def_en: having high moral qualities; grand, admirable; also, aristocratic
def_zh: noble six hundred, noble charge, noble deeds
quote_en: "When can their glory fade? / O the wild charge they made! / All the world wonder'd. / Honour the charge they made! / Honour the Light Brigade, / Noble six hundred!" – The poem concludes by exhorting the reader to honour the six hundred as noble, their glory eternal (The Charge of the Light Brigade, 1854)
quote_zh: Tennyson crowns the poem with ‘Noble’, capitalised implicitly by its position as the final epithet for the six hundred. The word fuses social class (officers and gentlemen) with moral quality (selfless courage), making the soldiers’ sacrifice a triumph of character over circumstance. The Latin root nobilis (known, famous) underscores that these men have become legendary through their action. The imperative ‘Honour the Light Brigade, / Noble six hundred!’ is a direct command to the reader, breaking the narrative frame and demanding participation in memory. ‘Noble’ sits within a semantic field of honour and glory that runs through the poem: ‘wonder’d’, ‘glory’, ‘hero’, ‘honour’. The open, long /oʊ/ vowel gives the word a spacious, lasting resonance, as if the word itself refuses to die. Tennyson, as Poet Laureate, uses ‘noble’ not as a description but as a public decree, an official bestowal of national gratitude that transforms military disaster into moral victory.
ex_en: The six hundred were noble, their wild and doomed charge a thing the world wondered at, and the poet commands that they be honoured.
ex_zh: The noblest thing he ever saw was not the charge itself but the moment after, when a single, riderless horse stood in the smoke, waiting for a command that would never come.

def_en: cylinders on which film or thread is wound; here, rolls of photographic film
def_zh: spools of suffering, winding spools, numbered spools
quote_en: "In his dark room he is finally alone / with spools of suffering set out in ordered rows." – The war photographer, now home in his darkroom, arranges the rolls of film that contain images of agony (War Photographer, 1985)
quote_zh: Duffy opens the poem with ‘spools’, an unremarkable technical object that the poem immediately charges with moral weight through the phrase ‘of suffering’. The word belongs to a semantic field of order and processing: ‘ordered rows’, ‘solutions slop’, ‘set out’. The plosive /p/ and the sibilant /s/ contrast, giving the word a soft yet precise sound, like the click of a film reel. By choosing ‘spools’ rather than ‘rolls’ or ‘canisters’, Duffy evokes the mechanical, repetitive nature of the photographer’s labour, which stands in grim contrast to the unrepeatable agony each image captures. The spools contain latent suffering, invisible until developed; the darkroom is a space where horror will be made visible again. The word also connotes the coiling and unwinding of memory—what has been wound onto the spool must be unwound in the mind.
ex_en: The photographer, alone at last, arranged the spools of film, each one coiled tight with images of suffering he had witnessed.
ex_zh: The spools in his darkroom were not just film; they were small, black coffins of time, each one holding a breath that had not yet been exhaled.

def_en: the colour of blood, fire, or rubies; associated with danger, passion, and sacrifice
def_zh: red light, red glow, red softly glows
quote_en: "The only light is red and softly glows, / as though this were a church and he / a priest preparing to intone a Mass." – The safelight in the darkroom is described as a religious red glow, sanctifying the space (War Photographer, 1985)
quote_zh: Duffy transforms the functional darkroom safelight into a symbol freighted with multiple meanings. ‘Red’ is the colour of blood, of the wounds the photographer has witnessed, but also of the sanctuary lamp that burns in a Catholic church, marking the presence of the sacred. This dual association immediately sets up the poem’s central metaphor: the darkroom as church, the photographer as priest. The word is placed at the line break, suspended momentarily before the verb ‘glows’, allowing the colour to pulse in the reader’s mind. The /r/ and /ɛ/ create a soft, reverent sound, while the alliteration with ‘roses’ (in ‘ordered rows’) and later ‘Rural England’ weaves a pattern of r’s that link the darkroom, the battlefield, and the home. Duffy’s choice of ‘red’ over ‘amber’ or ‘dim’ insists on the sacrificial, almost eucharistic quality of the photographer’s work—he is developing images of spilled blood in a room bathed in a blood-like light.
ex_en: The only light in the darkroom was red, glowing softly as though the room itself were a sacred space.
ex_zh: The red light in the darkroom was the same colour as the inside of his eyelids when he closed his eyes and saw, again, the dust road and the child’s shoe lying empty.

def_en: an ordained minister authorised to perform sacred rites; a mediator between the human and the divine
def_zh: priest preparing, as a priest, priest of photography
quote_en: "as though this were a church and he / a priest preparing to intone a Mass." – The photographer’s ritualised work is compared to that of a priest about to celebrate a liturgy (War Photographer, 1985)
quote_zh: Duffy uses ‘priest’ to elevate the photographer’s role while simultaneously questioning it. A priest mediates between the living and the divine; the photographer mediates between the suffering and the oblivious public. The word anchors a semantic field of religious ritual that runs through the poem: ‘church’, ‘Mass’, ‘intone’, and later, implicitly, ‘agonies’ (the Passion). The long /iː/ vowel gives the word a solemn, lingering quality. The comparison is not ironic but deeply ambivalent: the photographer does perform a kind of sacred duty, but his ‘Mass’ is the distribution of images to a public whose ‘eyeballs prick with tears between the bath and pre‑lunch beers’. Duffy, writing in 1985 with the televised famines and conflicts of the late twentieth century in mind, asks whether photography can ever truly be a sacrament, or whether it is merely a documentary trade. The priest is ‘preparing to intone’—the action is imminent, the ritual about to begin, and the reader is positioned as a reluctant congregant.
ex_en: The photographer stood in the red-lit room like a priest preparing to intone a Mass for the dead he had witnessed.
ex_zh: The priest of the darkroom performed his rites with chemicals and paper, but the congregation—the readers of the Sunday paper—never stayed long enough to receive the benediction.

def_en: with no special or distinctive features; normal, everyday, routine
def_zh: ordinary pain, ordinary life, return to ordinary
quote_en: "Home again to ordinary pain which simple weather can dispel, / to fields which don't explode beneath the feet / of running children in a nightmare heat." – The photographer returns to rural England, where suffering is mild and curable, unlike the war zones (War Photographer, 1985)
quote_zh: Duffy deploys ‘ordinary’ to expose the gulf between two worlds. Here, pain is ‘ordinary’—a headache, a grey day—and can be ‘dispel[led]’ by ‘simple weather’. The adjective belongs to a semantic field of the mundane that defines England in the poem: ‘Rural England’, ‘simple weather’, ‘fields’, ‘bath’, ‘pre‑lunch beers’. The contrast with the unspoken agony of the war zones is devastating. The phonetic flatness of ‘ordinary’—the unstressed first syllable, the clipped /ɔː/ and /dɪn/—mirrors the emotional flatness of the home front. Duffy’s choice of the word over ‘normal’ or ‘everyday’ carries a subtle judgement: the public lives in a state of ordinariness that the photographer can no longer share. He is ‘home again’ but cannot return to the ordinary; the spools of suffering have made him a stranger in his own land. The word also hints at the way suffering is apportioned: some pain is ordinary, some is unspeakable, and the line between is a matter of geography and luck.
ex_en: Back in rural England, he encountered only ordinary pain—the kind that a change in the weather could cure—in place of the extraordinary suffering he had left behind.
ex_zh: The ordinary silence of the English field was the loudest thing he had ever heard, because it was the absence of screaming.

def_en: extreme physical or mental suffering; intense pain
def_zh: hundred agonies, black-and-white agonies, agonies in photographs
quote_en: "A hundred agonies in black and white / from which his editor will pick out five or six / for Sunday's supplement." – The photographer's hundreds of images of suffering are reduced to a handful chosen by his editor (War Photographer, 1985)
quote_zh: Duffy uses ‘agonies’ as a blunt, quantifying noun—‘a hundred agonies’—that seems to count suffering like stock. The word is emotionally overwhelming in its plainness; it avoids euphemism and names the content of the photographs directly. The /æ/ and /ɡ/ sounds create a harsh, guttural texture, as if the word itself is wrung from the throat. ‘Agonies’ belongs to a semantic field of pain that runs through the poem: ‘suffering’, ‘cries’, ‘blood’, ‘nightmare heat’. The contrast with ‘black and white’ suggests both the literal monochrome of the photographs and the moral simplification imposed by the newspaper format. The editor’s selection of ‘five or six’ from a ‘hundred’ agonies is a brutal reduction, turning human catastrophe into a consumer choice. Duffy, a poet acutely aware of media ethics, critiques the way suffering is edited, cropped, and commodified for a Sunday readership. The photograph becomes a ‘supplement’, an addition to the newspaper and to the reader’s comfortable morning.
ex_en: The photographer had captured a hundred separate agonies on film, but the editor would select only a few, discarding the rest.
ex_zh: Each agony he printed was a name he did not know, and that not-knowing was its own quiet, persistent torment.

def_en: an additional section of a newspaper or magazine; something added to complete or enhance
def_zh: Sunday's supplement, colour supplement, magazine supplement
quote_en: "from which his editor will pick out five or six / for Sunday's supplement." – The chosen photographs will appear not in the main news pages but in a lifestyle magazine insert (War Photographer, 1985)
quote_zh: Duffy’s choice of ‘supplement’ is a precise, damning critique of how the media frames atrocity. A supplement is a magazine of leisure, fashion, and entertainment, inserted into the weekend paper. By placing the photographs of war there, the editor positions them alongside recipes and travel features, neutralising their moral force. The word’s Latin root, supplēre (to fill up), suggests that suffering is being used to fill column inches. The /s/ sibilance of ‘Sunday’s supplement’ is almost luxurious, a hiss of glossy paper that contrasts with the hard /ɡ/ of ‘agonies’. The word belongs to a semantic field of consumption and publication that includes ‘editor’, ‘reader’s eyeballs’, and ‘pick out’. The photographer’s work, which he performs with the solemnity of a priest, ends up as an adornment to a lazy Sunday. Duffy implies that the very format diminishes the reality it purports to show.
ex_en: The editor would pick five or six images of suffering to fill the pages of the Sunday supplement, a colourful magazine inserted among the headlines.
ex_zh: The supplement lay on the coffee table, the photograph of the dead soldier tucked between an advert for perfume and a recipe for roast lamb, and no one saw the red light that still glowed around it.

def_en: without showing emotion; calmly, unmoved
def_zh: stare impassively, watch impassively, gaze impassively
quote_en: "From the aeroplane he stares impassively at where / he earns his living and they do not care." – The photographer, departing again, looks down at the country that pays him but remains indifferent (War Photographer, 1985)
quote_zh: Duffy ends the poem with ‘impassively’, an adverb that captures the emotional deadlock at the heart of the photographer’s experience. To be impassive is to show no feeling, but the word does not necessarily mean feeling nothing; it suggests a suppression, a disciplined blankness. The /ɪm/ prefix is a negation, and the /pæs/ root (from Latin patī, to suffer) implies a refusal to suffer openly. The photographer, who has witnessed unthinkable suffering, now inhabits a state of emotional shutdown. The word sits in a semantic field of detachment and numbness that runs through the poem: ‘finally alone’, ‘hands which did not tremble then’, ‘he stares’. The closing couplet is devastating: ‘he earns his living and they do not care’. The public’s indifference is mirrored by the photographer’s impassivity, a necessary psychological armour that may have become permanent. Duffy’s placement of ‘impassively’ at the line break, before the enjambment to ‘he earns his living’, creates a pause that lets the reader inhabit the photographer’s frozen gaze. The adverb is not an accusation but a lament.
ex_en: From the aeroplane window, he stared impassively down at the fields of England, knowing that those below did not care where he had been or what he had seen.
ex_zh: He had trained himself to stare impassively at the lens of the world, but sometimes, in the darkroom, the red light saw him weeping.

def_en: an area planted with explosive mines; metaphorically, a situation fraught with hidden dangers
def_zh: minefield of memories, emotional minefield, city as minefield
quote_en: "He's back from a war / and the city sprawls / like a minefield of memories." – A veteran returns to civilian life, but the city streets feel as dangerous as a territory sown with hidden explosive recollections (Reservist, 1996)
quote_zh: Boey Kim Cheng uses ‘minefield’ to collapse the distance between a foreign war zone and the domestic city. A minefield is a space where a single step can trigger destruction; by mapping it onto ‘memories’, the poet shows that the veteran’s past is not behind him but everywhere, waiting to detonate. The word’s two syllables are a stark, military-sounding compound—hard /m/, long /aɪ/, then the thud of /fiːld/—that brings the battlefield into the civilian world. It belongs to a semantic field of hidden threat and explosion, echoed later by ‘dustbins clatter / like gunfire’. The simile ‘like a minefield of memories’ transforms the entire urban landscape into a psychological hazard. Unlike a poet who might speak of ‘floods’ or ‘shadows’, Boey Kim Cheng chooses a specifically modern, industrial weapon, underscoring that the war the veteran fought is not a distant, chivalric affair but a contemporary hell of improvised explosive devices. The city ‘sprawls’, an ungainly verb that mirrors the veteran’s disorientation: there is no straight path, only potential eruptions of memory.
ex_en: The city that once was home now sprawls before the returned soldier like a minefield, every street corner a potential explosion of memory.
ex_zh: Every laugh from a passing stranger was a step into that minefield, and he had learned to walk without lifting his feet, sliding forward as if the ground itself might give way.

def_en: moves regularly around an area to keep watch or guard; here, a nocturnal mental vigilance
def_zh: patrols the corridors, patrols at night, patrols sleep
quote_en: "At night he patrols / the corridors of sleep," – The veteran, in slumber, moves through his own dreams as if on a military patrol (Reservist, 1996)
quote_zh: The verb ‘patrols’ transforms sleep from a state of rest into an active, defensive operation. The military register is precise: the veteran does not wander or dream; he patrols, carrying his duty into the unconscious. The /p/ and /tr/ sounds are crisp and purposeful, evoking a soldier moving with caution. The word anchors a semantic field of surveillance and alertness: ‘sentry’, ‘watching’, ‘listening’, ‘coded messages’ all reinforce the idea that for the veteran, peace is merely a different kind of war. By using ‘corridors of sleep’, Boey Kim Cheng spatialises the psyche into a building or base that must be secured, room by room. The enjambment at ‘sleep’ suspends the line, mimicking the suspension of rest; the sentence continues, and the patrol never ends. The poet suggests that the veteran’s trauma has militarised even his innermost self, leaving no sanctuary uninvaded.
ex_en: At night, the veteran patrols the corridors of his own sleep, never truly resting, always on watch.
ex_zh: He patrolled the quiet house at 3 a.m., not because he expected an intruder, but because sleep itself had become a territory that required his constant, exhausted vigilance.

def_en: a soldier stationed to keep guard or control access to a place
def_zh: sentry on sentry duty, standing sentry, sentry in the dark
quote_en: "a sentry on sentry duty / in the dark, watching / for movements in the shadows," – The veteran, even in sleep, is on guard, a double layer of sentry duty: one for his body, one for his mind (Reservist, 1996)
quote_zh: The repetition in ‘sentry on sentry duty’ creates a haunting redundancy, as if the veteran is guarding himself guarding nothing. A sentry is a lookout against external threats, but here the enemy is internal—‘movements in the shadows’ within his own mind. The /s/ and /tr/ sounds are sharp and secretive, mimicking whispered passwords. The word belongs to a semantic field of military protection that ironically offers no real protection at all; the sentry is watching for a foe that has already infiltrated. The phrase ‘in the dark’ literalises the unknown, but also the veteran’s blindness to his own condition. Boey Kim Cheng may be echoing the condition of hypervigilance in PTSD (Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder), where the body remains in combat mode long after the war is over. The sentry is not a figure of strength but of exhausted compulsion, unable to stand down.
ex_en: Even in the dark of his own room, the veteran remains a sentry, watching for shadows that move with the memory of an enemy.
ex_zh: The sentry in his brain never received the order to stand down; it is still there, forty years later, asking every leaf that taps the window for its name and serial number.

def_en: converted into a secret language or system of signals; carrying a hidden meaning
def_zh: coded messages, coded wires, coded hum
quote_en: "The telephone wires hum / with coded messages." – The ordinary sound of telephone lines becomes, for the veteran, a secret military communication (Reservist, 1996)
quote_zh: ‘Coded’ transforms a mundane, neutral sound—the hum of telephone wires—into an item of intelligence, a message only the veteran can hear. The word suggests encryption, secrecy, and a world in which nothing is innocent. The /k/ sound is sharp and hard, cutting off the softer hum. The adjective belongs to a semantic field of surveillance and paranoia: the wires hum, the dustbins clatter like gunfire, the shadows conceal movements. The veteran’s mind has become a decoder of threat, assigning hostile significance to every ambient noise. Boey Kim Cheng, writing in a post‑war Singaporean context, may also be drawing on the experience of generations for whom conflict and military preparedness were a constant, low‑level background. The telephone wire—something domestic—carries the war into the home. The ‘coded messages’ are never decoded; the poem does not tell us what they say, because the real message is the condition itself: the veteran is trapped in a frequency of fear that the civilian world cannot tune into.
ex_en: The telephone wires outside his window hum with coded messages that only the veteran, with his trained ears, can detect.
ex_zh: Every creak of the floorboard was a coded message in a language he had thought he had forgotten, but which his spine still translated into adrenaline.

def_en: the repeated firing of guns; a sound of battle
def_zh: clatter like gunfire, sound of gunfire, distant gunfire
quote_en: "The dustbins clatter / like gunfire." – The ordinary sound of dustbins being moved triggers the veteran’s memory of shooting (Reservist, 1996)
quote_zh: ‘Gunfire’ is the climax of the poem’s soundscape, a word that collapses the safe present into the traumatic past in an instant. The hard /ɡ/ and the double /f/ produce a sharp, percussive effect, like a round being discharged. The simile ‘clatter like gunfire’ reveals a process of auditory hallucination: a neutral, everyday sound is converted by the veteran’s brain into a threat signal. The word belongs to a semantic field of warfare and sudden noise that includes ‘minefield’, ‘rustle of the enemy’, ‘coded messages’, and ‘explodes’ (implicitly). Boey Kim Cheng draws on the well‑documented clinical phenomenon of exaggerated startle response in PTSD, but he renders it poetically: the gunfire is not just a memory but a present, sensory reality. The dustbins—unseen, ordinary, municipal—become the enemy artillery. The brevity of the line, three words, mimics the suddenness of the sound and the abruptness of the veteran’s terror.
ex_en: The dustbins clattered outside, and for a split second the veteran heard not rubbish collection but the gunfire of his past.
ex_zh: The gunfire never stopped; it just learned to disguise itself as slamming doors, bursting tyres, and the sharp laugh of a stranger in a restaurant.

def_en: moisture exuded through the pores, especially from heat or anxiety
def_zh: in a sweat, cold sweat, pool of sweat
quote_en: "He wakes in a sweat, / a pool of cold sweat / on the sheet," – The veteran wakes in physical distress, his body drenched in the moisture of fear (Reservist, 1996)
quote_zh: ‘Sweat’ is the poem’s most visceral, bodily word, grounding the veteran’s psychological ordeal in a physical reality. The word is blunt, monosyllabic, wet; its /sw/ onset mimics a sudden wash. Unlike ‘perspiration’, ‘sweat’ carries connotations of labour, sickness, and fear—it is the body’s honest confession. The image of a ‘pool of cold sweat’ is paradoxical: a pool suggests containment, but cold sweat is a symptom of shock, the body’s fight-or-flight response. The adjective ‘cold’ turns the sweat from a sign of warmth and life into a sign of terror and death. The word belongs to a semantic field of bodily reaction that the poem has built from the start: the veteran patrols, watches, listens, and his body finally speaks in this soaking. It also prefigures the final image of ‘an island of fear / in a sea of peace’, with the sweat being the wet medium of his isolation. Boey Kim Cheng does not allow the veteran any heroic stoicism; the sweat is an involuntary marker of ongoing suffering.
ex_en: Each night, the veteran woke in a cold sweat, the sheets a pool of his body’s memory of terror.
ex_zh: The sweat on his skin in the morning was the only map of the night’s battles, and his wife, who had learned to read it, touched his forehead softly without a word.

def_en: an unpleasant emotion caused by the threat of danger, pain, or harm
def_zh: island of fear, pool of fear, cold sweat of fear
quote_en: "an island of fear / in a sea of peace." – The veteran’s mental state is a small, isolated pocket of terror surrounded by the seemingly calm world of peacetime (Reservist, 1996)
quote_zh: The poem closes on ‘fear’, a naked, unmodified noun that names the veteran’s entire condition. The metaphor ‘island of fear’ turns an emotion into a geography of isolation. Fear is not a passing feeling but a territory he inhabits, cut off from the ‘sea of peace’ that surrounds him but cannot reach him. The /f/ and /ɪə/ diphthong are breathy and hollow, like an exhalation. The word belongs to a semantic field of psychological suffering that contrasts starkly with the external world: the city sprawls, people go about their day, but the veteran is marooned in his own alertness. The final juxtaposition is devastating: a ‘sea of peace’—vast, fluid, available to all—yet the veteran is stuck on his island, unable to swim. Boey Kim Cheng draws on the Singaporean context of a nation in peace, with mandatory military service (National Service) that may leave reservists with unprocessed trauma. The poem’s whole arc has led to this word: the minefield, the patrols, the sentry, the gunfire, the sweat—all are manifestations of this island of fear. The peace is real, but the fear is equally real, and the poem refuses to reconcile them.
ex_en: Despite the peacetime city around him, the veteran remained trapped on an island of fear, isolated from the calm that others enjoyed.
ex_zh: The peace he had been promised was not a sea but a thin sheet of ice over a lake of fear, and he spent every day waiting for the crack.

def_en: a small measure of alcoholic drink, roughly a half-pint or a small mug
def_zh: wet my nipperkin, share a nipperkin, old nipperkin
quote_en: "Had he and I but met / By some old ancient inn, / We should have sat us down to wet / Right many a nipperkin!" – The speaker imagines a scenario where he and the enemy soldier might have shared drinks together as friends (The Man He Killed, 1902)
quote_zh: Hardy opens with the obsolete, dialect word ‘nipperkin’ to evoke a vanishing rural England of friendly inns and shared ale. The word’s archaic, almost nursery-rhyme quality—the tripping /nɪpər/ followed by the diminutive /kɪn/—creates a tone of wistful, humble conviviality. By choosing this homely term rather than ‘drink’ or ‘pint’, Hardy anchors the speaker in a rustic, non-military identity; he is a farmhand or labourer, not a soldier by trade. The word anchors a semantic field of domestic comfort and fellowship—‘inn’, ‘sat us down’, ‘wet’, ‘ancient’—that exists only in the subjunctive mood of ‘Had he and I but met’. The nipperkin symbolises a peaceable life denied by war. In the poem’s circular logic, the nipperkin is the counterfactual that makes the killing all the more senseless.
ex_en: The speaker reflects that if he had met the man he killed in civilian life, they would have shared many a nipperkin together in an old inn.
ex_zh: The nipperkin stood empty on the bar, a small monument to all the men who might have drunk together had the world not decided they were enemies.

def_en: to enroll in the armed forces; to join up
def_zh: 'list at ease, 'list for war, just 'list
quote_en: "I shot him dead because— / Because he was my foe, / Just so: my foe of course he was; / That's clear enough; although // He thought he'd 'list, perhaps," – The speaker explains he killed the man simply because he was the enemy, then reflects that the man probably enlisted for the same casual reasons as himself (The Man He Killed, 1902)
quote_zh: Hardy uses the contraction ‘'list’ to indicate spontaneous, casual speech, as if the speaker is talking in a pub rather than making a formal statement. The apostrophe signals a colloquial dropping of the syllable, making the word sound offhand and unheroic. The verb ‘enlist’ (to join the army) is stripped down to its barest form, mirroring the speaker’s vague, unglamorous reasons for becoming a soldier. The word sits within a semantic field of casual motivation and lack of agency: ‘no other reason why’, ‘off-hand like’, ‘just as I’. Hardy, who opposed the Boer War (during which this poem was written), shows how ordinary men become combatants not through ideology or patriotism but through vague economic or social pressures. The hesitation in the stanza—the speaker trails off after ‘'list, perhaps,’—mimics the difficulty of finding a logical justification for killing a man who ‘thought he'd 'list’ just like himself.
ex_en: The man he killed had probably enlisted for the same off-hand reasons he himself had, without deep conviction.
ex_zh: The young soldier didn't march to war; he just 'listed, as one might 'list for a cricket team, and never imagined the other side had lads doing exactly the same.

def_en: an enemy, especially in battle
def_zh: face my foe, enemy foe, foe of course
quote_en: "I shot him dead because— / Because he was my foe, / Just so: my foe of course he was; / That's clear enough;" – The speaker states the official reason for the killing: the man was designated an enemy (The Man He Killed, 1902)
