quote_zh: Hardy places ‘foe’ at the core of the poem’s exploration of the absurdity of conflict. The word is archaic and poetic, distancing the enemy as something from a story or ballad rather than a real man. The repetition—‘Because he was my foe’—shows the speaker grasping for justification, as if stating the definition will be enough. The /f/ and /oʊ/ sounds are soft and hollow, lacking the aggression of ‘enemy’ or ‘adversary’. The word belongs to a semantic field of imposed identity that the poem dismantles: ‘foe’, ‘enemy’, ‘other’ are labels that collapse when the speaker imagines the man as a fellow ‘nipperkin’-drinker. The stammering ‘because— / Because’ before ‘foe’ reveals a mind struggling to reconcile the official reason with the human reality, and the sardonic ‘Just so: my foe of course’ suggests an unspoken doubt. For Hardy, ‘foe’ is a category created by war, not by individual grievance.
ex_en: The speaker killed the man because he was officially his foe, but the word felt hollow as soon as he thought about the man's life.
ex_zh: On the battlefield, foes were made by geography; in the grave, geography was made by foes, all laid together in a line that no map would ever claim.

def_en: personal belongings or baggage; kit, things
def_zh: traps of war, bring no traps, leave his traps
quote_en: "He thought he'd 'list, perhaps, / Off-hand like—just as I— / Was out of work—had sold his traps— / No other reason why." – The speaker speculates that the enemy soldier enlisted for the same mundane reasons: unemployment and the selling off of his possessions (The Man He Killed, 1902)
quote_zh: Hardy uses the colloquial ‘traps’ to suggest the meagre possessions of a poor working man—tools, clothes, bits of kit—that he had to sell before enlisting out of economic necessity. The word’s association with animal trapping also hints at a life tied to the land, to a rural existence of labour and hardship. The /tr/ and /æps/ sounds are rough and unpolished, matching the speaker’s plain voice. ‘Traps’ belongs to a semantic field of poverty and lack of choice that Hardy carefully constructs: ‘out of work’, ‘sold’, ‘no other reason’. The act of selling one’s ‘traps’ is a stripping of civilian identity before entering the army, a small, personal bankruptcy. Hardy, writing in the aftermath of the Boer War, critiques the economic pressures that made men enlist not for glory but for survival. The word humanises the enemy: he, too, was a man who owned so little that he could carry everything in a bundle and sell it.
ex_en: The enemy soldier, like the speaker, had likely been out of work and had sold his traps—his few belongings—before enlisting for no grander reason.
ex_zh: The traps he sold before he marched were a tin kettle, a clasp knife, and a woman's photograph, and the buyer at the market stall never knew he was holding a man's entire life.

def_en: strange, odd, peculiar; also, inquisitive
def_zh: curious thing, curious war, curious wonder
quote_en: "Yes; quaint and curious war is! / You shoot a fellow down / You'd treat if met where any bar is, / Or help to half-a-crown." – The speaker concludes that war is a strange and peculiar business, where you kill a man you would otherwise buy a drink (The Man He Killed, 1902)
quote_zh: Hardy selects ‘curious’ to characterise war not as monstrous or glorious but as bafflingly strange. The word belongs to a register of mild, understated puzzlement—‘quaint and curious’—that contrasts deliberately with the violence of ‘shoot a fellow down’. This bathos, this deliberate deflation, is Hardy’s chief rhetorical strategy: war is not epic but absurd. The /kj/ and /ʊə/ sounds are almost whimsical, as if the speaker is scratching his head. The word anchors the poem’s concluding irony: war is a social arrangement that makes killing an obligation and treating a man to a drink a crime of fraternisation. The pairing of ‘treat’ (buy a drink) and ‘half-a-crown’ (a coin) returns to the imagery of the inn, closing the poem’s circle. The ‘curious’ nature of war is not that it is strange but that it has been normalised; the speaker is the only one who seems to find it odd. For Hardy, the word is a quiet indictment of a society that accepts this curiosity without question.
ex_en: War, the speaker reflects, is a quaint and curious thing: you shoot a man you would have gladly stood a drink in any pub.
ex_zh: The curious thing was not the bullet hole in the photograph, but that the photograph had been in the pocket of a man whose name he never learned, and yet he felt he had known him well.

def_en: shockingly frightful or horrible; deathly pale
def_zh: ghastly suit, ghastly grey, ghastly face
quote_en: "He sat in a wheeled chair, waiting for dark, / And shivered in his ghastly suit of grey," – A disabled veteran sits in a wheelchair, cold and waiting, wearing hospital clothes the colour of death (Disabled, 1917)
quote_zh: Owen opens with ‘ghastly’, an adjective that layers physical appearance, emotional horror, and supernatural dread. The word derives from the Old English gǣstan (to terrify), and its ghostly roots linger: the soldier is like a spectre, already half-dead. The ‘suit of grey’ is a hospital uniform, but Owen pushes the colour into a semantic field of death and pallor—‘grey’, ‘dark’, ‘pale’, ‘shivered’, ‘cold’—that paints the veteran as a living corpse. The /ɡɑːst/ syllable is drawn out, almost gagging, followed by the softer /li/, creating a rhythm of disgust and pity. Owen chose ‘ghastly’ over ‘drab’ or ‘dull’ because it carries moral judgement: what society has done to this man is ghastly. The word also contrasts violently with the warm, colourful past he will remember—‘glow’, ‘girls’, ‘blood’—making the present all the more obscene.
ex_en: The soldier sat shivering in his hospital wheelchair, wrapped in a ghastly grey suit, like a ghost waiting for permission to leave.
ex_zh: The ghastly thing was not the missing legs, but the smile he pasted on whenever visitors came, a smile that had learned to lie long before his body had learned to fail.

def_en: fitted with wheels; moved or transported on wheels
def_zh: wheeled chair, wheeled into place, wheeled away
quote_en: "He sat in a wheeled chair, waiting for dark, / And shivered in his ghastly suit of grey," – The veteran sits in a wheelchair, immobile and dependent (Disabled, 1917)
quote_zh: The adjective ‘wheeled’ transforms the chair from furniture into a permanent, defining condition. The soldier does not simply use a wheelchair; he inhabits the ‘wheeled chair’, as if the chair has become his body. The /wiːld/ sound echoes ‘wielded’, suggesting something once handled by others—the chair is an object pushed, steered, or parked to wait. The word anchors a semantic field of immobility and passivity that runs throughout the poem: ‘sat’, ‘waiting’, ‘shivered’, ‘leaning’, ‘carried’. Owen, himself invalided home from the front, writes with bitter knowledge of the wards full of amputees. The chair is not a symbol of recovery but of stasis. The alliteration with ‘waiting’ and the enjambment into ‘for dark’ makes the waiting endless, the dark the only release.
ex_en: The soldier, now legless, spent his days in a wheeled chair, waiting for the dark that would hide him from the world.
ex_zh: The wheeled chair creaked in the hospital corridor like an unoiled axle, and each turn of its wheels was a slow, circular journey back to the moment the shell had spun him into a different life.

def_en: shook slightly and uncontrollably, especially from cold, fear, or shock
def_zh: shivered in the cold, shivered and waited, shivered with fear
quote_en: "He sat in a wheeled chair, waiting for dark, / And shivered in his ghastly suit of grey," – The veteran trembles with cold and, implicitly, with trauma (Disabled, 1917)
quote_zh: Owen’s choice of ‘shivered’ is devastating in its ambiguity. The verb describes a physical response to cold—the thin hospital suit offers no warmth—but it also implies a deeper, involuntary tremor of trauma. The /ʃ/ sound is breathy and shaking, as if the word itself is vibrating. The past tense places the shivering as a continuous state: he sat and he shivered, over and over. The word belongs to a semantic field of cold and vulnerability alongside ‘ghastly’, ‘grey’, ‘cold’, and later ‘waiting for dark’. The shivering contrasts explicitly with the remembered past, where his limbs were whole and his body generated its own heat from dancing and football. Now, with severed legs, his body cannot warm itself; circulation is poor, the stumps ache, and the mind replays the shell-burst that took everything. Owen, who served in the Manchesters, saw the wreckage of bodies that survived but never recovered.
ex_en: The legless soldier shivered in his grey hospital suit, waiting in the cold for the darkness that might bring sleep.
ex_zh: The cold that made him shiver was not the autumn wind slipping through the ward’s cracked window but the memory of the exact instant when the shell’s hot metal met his thighs.

def_en: the red liquid circulating in the body; a symbol of life, sacrifice, or violence
def_zh: smear of blood, blood of boys, shed blood
quote_en: "About this time Town used to swing so gay / When glow-lamps budded in the light-blue trees, / And girls glanced lovelier as the air grew dim,— / In the old times, before he threw away his knees. / Now he will never feel again how slim / Girls' waists are, or how warm their subtle hands; / All of them touch him like some queer disease. / One time he liked a blood-smear down his leg," – The veteran remembers, with bitter irony, how he once was proud of a minor football injury showing on his skin (Disabled, 1917)
quote_zh: The word ‘blood’ in this passage is charged with savage irony. In peacetime, a blood-smear from sport was a badge of masculinity, a trivial wound that attracted admiration from girls. Now, the blood shed by the soldier has cost him his legs and his future. Owen places ‘blood-smear’ in the remembered, pre-war world of ‘glow-lamps’ and ‘girls’, making it a token of innocence, even vanity. The direct, unadorned noun ‘blood’ condenses the life-force that the soldier has permanently lost; what once was a sign of vitality is now a sign of absence. The word anchors a semantic field of bodily ruin that the poem catalogues: ‘threw away his knees’, ‘legless’, ‘sewn short at elbow’, ‘half his lifetime lapsed’. The contrast between the ‘blood-smear’ of a football graze and the haemorrhage of a battlefield amputation is the poem’s unspoken horror.
ex_en: In happier times, the young soldier had been proud of a blood-smear on his leg after a football match; now that same leg was gone, and the blood was real.
ex_zh: The blood he once shed playing sport was a promise he made to the future; the blood that left him on the Somme was a debt the past would never stop collecting.

def_en: the joint between the thigh and lower leg; metaphorically, youth, mobility, and manhood
def_zh: threw away his knees, shattered knees, no knees
quote_en: "In the old times, before he threw away his knees." – The veteran reminisces about the time before he lost his legs in war (Disabled, 1917)
quote_zh: Owen’s phrase ‘threw away his knees’ is a masterstroke of understatement and violent metaphor. The verb ‘threw away’ suggests something casual, wasteful, like discarding a bus ticket, while ‘knees’ stands metonymically for the soldier’s entire manhood and mobility. The /niːz/ sound is sharp, almost plaintive, ending with a hiss. The word belongs to a semantic field of physical loss and regret: ‘legless’, ‘half his lifetime’, and the later ‘sewn short at elbow’. Knees are joints, agents of movement, of football, of dancing, of kneeling to propose; to lose them is to lose the future. Owen does not specify how the knees were lost—shell, bullet, surgeon’s saw—because the poem’s focus is not on the moment of injury but on its lifelong aftermath. The choice of ‘knees’ rather than ‘legs’ is specific: knees are where the body bends, where prayer happens, where a man goes down on one knee before a woman.
ex_en: The young man had once been an athlete, but he threw away his knees in battle, and now he could neither walk nor kneel.
ex_zh: The knees he lost were not just bone and ligament; they were the last dance he never danced, the goal he never scored, the proposal he never made.

def_en: perhaps; possibly; expressing uncertainty
def_zh: maybe he noticed, maybe he didn't, maybe they'd see
quote_en: "Someone had said he'd look a god in kilts, / That's why; and maybe, too, to please his Meg;" – The speaker speculates that the soldier enlisted partly for vanity, to look good in a uniform, and perhaps to impress his girlfriend (Disabled, 1917)
quote_zh: Owen’s use of ‘maybe’ reveals the awful, trivial uncertainty that led men to enlist and lose their limbs. The adverb is tentative, casual, and domestic, belonging to a discourse of small choices rather than life-altering decisions. The word anchors a semantic field of vague, inadequate motivation: ‘someone had said’, ‘that’s why’, ‘to please his Meg’. Owen, writing against the jingoistic recruitment rhetoric of his time, exposes how young men were flattered and cajoled into uniform by notions of glamour and masculinity. The ‘maybe’ is devastating because it remains unanswered; the soldier himself no longer knows why he went. The long /eɪ/ vowel in ‘maybe’ sounds like a shrug. The word sits as a pause in the narrative, a moment of reflection that the reader must sit with, realising that a man’s entire life has been undone by a ‘maybe’.
ex_en: The soldier did not enlist out of deep patriotism but maybe to impress his girlfriend and maybe because someone said he would look good in uniform.
ex_zh: Every day, in the ward, he played the game of maybe: maybe if he had waited a week, maybe if he had listened to his mother, maybe if the shell had landed three feet to the left.

def_en: a feeling of pride, fellowship, and common loyalty shared by members of a group
def_zh: military esprit de corps, shared esprit de corps, young esprit de corps
quote_en: "He thought of jewelled hilts / For daggers in plaid socks; of smart salutes; / And care of arms; and leave; and pay arrears; / Esprit de corps; and hints for young recruits." – The soldier remembers the camaraderie and military pride that briefly filled him with a sense of belonging (Disabled, 1917)
quote_zh: Owen inserts the French phrase ‘esprit de corps’ into a list of naive, pre-war enthusiasms—dress weapons, salutes, and the glamour of army life. The phrase is a term of art, meaning the spirit of the unit, the collective morale that binds soldiers together. By placing it among trivial attractions like ‘pay arrears’ and ‘jewelled hilts’, Owen undercuts its nobility; the esprit de corps that recruiting posters promised was just another advertisement for the slaughterhouse. The French pronunciation, preserved in the English text, adds an air of sophistication, a borrowed glamour, that the reality of the trenches would obliterate. The word belongs to a semantic field of military illusion and recruitment seduction. The veteran now, legless and waiting for dark, can only remember the phrase as an abstraction that cost him his body. Owen may be drawing on his own complex relationship with the regiment: he loved his men but loathed the system that consumed them.
ex_en: The young recruit had been seduced by the idea of esprit de corps, the belonging and pride of the regiment, never imagining he would belong only to a ward of amputees.
ex_zh: The esprit de corps he had loved was a warm, singing fire that burned for three summers; then the fire took his youth and left only the ash-grey suit he wore now.

def_en: a feeling of sorrow and compassion caused by the suffering of others
def_zh: pass for pity, ask for pity, receive pity
quote_en: "Now, he will spend a few sick years in Institutes, / And do what things the rules consider wise, / And take whatever pity they may dole." – The veteran faces a future in care institutions, receiving only the patronising pity that staff and visitors might offer (Disabled, 1917)
quote_zh: Owen ends the poem’s third-to-last stanza on ‘pity’, a word that sums up the veteran’s entire future. Pity is not love, not respect, not justice; it is a condescending handout, something ‘doled’ like a charity coin. The word’s /pɪ/ is thin and pinched, and the /ti/ fades into silence. Owen, deeply influenced by his work at Craiglockhart Hospital, knew that pity was the only emotion society was willing to give its damaged soldiers—and that pity felt, to the recipient, like a second wound. The verb ‘dole’ reinforces the sense of grudging charity. The word belongs to a semantic field of institutionalisation and diminishment: ‘Institutes’, ‘rules consider wise’, ‘dole’, ‘patient’. The poem’s final image of the veteran asking for a ‘pity’ as one might ask for a meal is a deliberate echo of the earlier, vigorous life of football, girls, and blood-smear. Owen’s anti-war argument culminates not in a scream but in this quiet, devastating word: the hero becomes a pitiable object.
ex_en: The veteran, now legless and institutionalised, will receive only the pity that the rules allow to be given, and nothing that resembles a full life.
ex_zh: Pity was a cold coin that strangers dropped into his lap, and he was expected to say thank you, though its edges cut his palms every time he closed his hand.

def_en: having no hair on the head; lacking natural covering; blatant, undisguised
def_zh: bald and short of breath, bald Majors, bald lie
quote_en: "If I were fierce, and bald, and short of breath, / I'd live with scarlet Majors at the Base," – The speaker imagines himself as a typical base officer: middle-aged, balding, physically unfit for combat (Base Details, 1917)
quote_zh: Sassoon opens with 'bald' as the first visible marker of the officer class he is satirising. Baldness here is not just a physical trait but a signifier of age, safety, and detachment from the young men dying at the front. The word is blunt and unadorned, the /b/ and /ɔːld/ sounds almost comically plain, stripping the officer of any heroic dignity. It belongs to a semantic field of physical decay and softness—'short of breath', 'puffy', 'petulant'—that contrasts with the 'glum heroes' who are young and doomed. By placing 'bald' first in the list, Sassoon suggests that those who send soldiers to death are themselves past their prime, clinging to life while youth is sacrificed. The bald head is also a symbol of the bare, unadorned truth that the poem exposes: these men, comfortable and safe, are morally naked.
ex_en: The speaker imagines being a bald, short-of-breath officer, living in comfort at the base while sending young heroes to their deaths.
ex_zh: The general's bald head gleamed under the chandelier at the hotel, as if the light itself was trying to point out the man who had sent so many into the dark.

def_en: a brilliant red colour; the colour of British staff officers' uniforms
def_zh: scarlet Majors, scarlet tabs, scarlet uniform
quote_en: "If I were fierce, and bald, and short of breath, / I'd live with scarlet Majors at the Base," – The speaker imagines himself among the red-tabbed staff officers who work far behind the front line (Base Details, 1917)
quote_zh: 'Scarlet' is the colour of the gorget tabs worn by British staff officers, a detail that would have been immediately recognised by Sassoon's wartime readership. The word's vivid, luxurious sound—the hissing /s/ and the liquid /ɑːr/ and /l/—suggests privilege and ostentation. Scarlet is the colour of blood, but these Majors wear it on their collars, not on their wounds. The word anchors a semantic field of decorative authority and distance from the actual fighting: 'at the Base', 'best hotel', 'toddle safely home'. Sassoon, who knew the staff system as a decorated infantry officer, uses 'scarlet' to draw a sharp line between the glamour of command and the mud of the trenches. The Majors are 'scarlet'—showy, theatrical—while the soldiers they dispatch remain invisible, 'glum' and soon 'stone dead'. The colour becomes a symbol of a war fought from armchairs.
ex_en: The staff officers at the base wore scarlet collar tabs, bright badges of their distance from the gunfire.
ex_zh: The scarlet on the Major's collar was not the red of poppies but the red of a butcher's apron that had never been to the slaughterhouse.

def_en: looking or feeling morose, dejected, and sullen
def_zh: glum heroes, glum faces, glum soldiers
quote_en: "And speed glum heroes up the line to death." – The base officers send young soldiers, described as dejected heroes, towards the front to die (Base Details, 1917)
quote_zh: Sassoon uses 'glum' to undercut the word 'heroes' with devastating economy. The heroes of this war are not radiant or triumphant; they are 'glum'—morose, silent, and resigned. The /ɡl/ and /ʌm/ sounds are heavy and downturned, mimicking the droop of a mouth. The word satirises the gap between official rhetoric and grimy reality: the officers call them heroes, but they are merely boys trying not to cry. 'Glum' belongs to a semantic field of dejection and weariness that the poem implicitly contrasts with the officers' 'puffy' indulgence. The alliteration with 'guzzling' (later) links the two words by sound alone, as if the officers' consumption is the answer to the soldiers' gloom. The verb 'speed' is cruel—the officers expedite death as if it were a bureaucratic process, and the heroes are too 'glum' to protest.
ex_en: The young soldiers, glum and silent, were sent up the line to die by officers who called them heroes and themselves ate well.
ex_zh: The glum line of soldiers on the road to the front was the longest poem Sassoon ever saw, and all its rhymes were the sounds of boots and rain.

def_en: swollen, bloated; soft and inflated
def_zh: puffy face, puffy petulant, puffy cheeks
quote_en: "You'd see me with my puffy petulant face," – The speaker imagines his own face as bloated and irritably childish, a result of overindulgence (Base Details, 1917)
quote_zh: 'Puffy' is a brilliantly dismissive adjective that renders the officer's face infantile and repulsive. The word suggests a bloatedness from gluttony and drink—the 'guzzling and gulping' that follows—but also a softness that contrasts with the lean, haggard faces of the fighting men. The alliteration with 'petulant' amplifies the childishness; the officer is not dignified but sulky, a pouting child in a scarlet uniform. The /p/ and /f/ sounds are soft and plosive, creating an aural impression of something inflated and empty. The word belongs to a semantic field of physical indulgence and moral flabbiness that Sassoon constructs: 'puffy', 'guzzling', 'gulping', 'best hotel'. This is the body of a man who has never gone without, and yet he presides over the deprivation and death of others. The face is a map of the officer's soul: swollen with self-importance, puffed up with minor power.
ex_en: The officer's face was puffy and petulant, a doughy mask of overfeeding and underserving.
ex_zh: The puffy face stared back at him from the mirror each morning, and he slapped his own cheeks until they reddened, pretending it was shame and not the sting of his palm.

def_en: eating or drinking greedily, often noisily
def_zh: guzzling and gulping, guzzling at the hotel, guzzling officers
quote_en: "Guzzling and gulping in the best hotel," – The officers are described eating and drinking greedily in a luxurious hotel behind the lines (Base Details, 1917)
quote_zh: Sassoon pairs 'guzzling' with 'gulping' to create an image of animal consumption, a feeding devoid of manners or mindfulness. The word's /ɡʌz/ sound is harsh and gurgling, mimicking the noise of greedy swallowing. The verb is usually applied to pigs or gluttons; by assigning it to officers, Sassoon strips them of any pretense to refinement. The word anchors a semantic field of consumption and corpulence: 'guzzling', 'gulping', 'best hotel', and implicitly the 'puffy' body that results. The officers eat while the 'glum heroes' go hungry, drink fine wine while soldiers drink tainted water. The present participle '-ing' suggests an ongoing, habitual action—this is what they do, day after day, as the casualty lists grow. The 'best hotel' is a world apart from the trench, a space of indulgence that exists in obscene proximity to the front.
ex_en: While young soldiers trudged towards the line, the officers could be found guzzling and gulping their meals in the best hotel behind the lines.
ex_zh: The guzzling in the hotel had stopped only once, when a waiter dropped a tray and every Major flinched as if a shell had landed—then the laughter resumed, and so did the wine.

def_en: a small piece; a fragment; a fight or skirmish, especially a minor one
def_zh: this last scrap, bloody scrap, a little scrap
quote_en: "'I used to know his father well; / Yes, we've lost heavily in this last scrap.'" – The officer, reading the casualty list, dismisses a major battle as a mere 'scrap' (Base Details, 1917)
quote_zh: 'Scrap' is the poem's most damning word, a piece of slang that reduces mass slaughter to the scale of a schoolyard fistfight. The word's shortness—a blunt monosyllable ending in a hard /p/—is almost contemptuous. By putting this euphemism in the officer's own mouth, Sassoon allows him to condemn himself. The word belongs to a semantic field of trivialisation and comfortable distance: 'poor young chap', 'I used to know his father well', 'we've lost heavily'—all clichés that insulate the speaker from the reality of what 'lost' means. A scrap is something minor, momentary, forgotten. The officer's casual 'we' includes himself in the 'loss', though he has lost nothing but a few names on a Roll of Honour. The contrast between 'heavily' and 'scrap' is jarring: a heavy loss in a minor fight is logically absurd, but the officer does not notice the contradiction. Sassoon, who fought at the Somme, knew that such language was the soundtrack of the war from the rear.
ex_en: The officer, scanning the death count, referred to the bloody battle as 'this last scrap', as if it were a football match.
ex_zh: The word 'scrap' fell from the officer's lips and landed on the table like a bone, and everyone pretended not to see the marrow of the dead it still carried.

def_en: to walk with short, unsteady steps, like a young child or an old person
def_zh: toddle home, toddle safely, toddle to bed
quote_en: "And when the war is done and youth stone dead, / I'd toddle safely home and die—in bed." – The speaker imagines the officer, after the war, waddling home to a peaceful death of old age (Base Details, 1917)
quote_zh: Sassoon ends the poem with 'toddle', a verb that transforms the officer into a grotesque infant. Toddling is how babies walk, unsteady and self-absorbed, or how the elderly shuffle toward their final years. The word's /tɒd/ and /l/ sounds are light and almost comical, a nursery rhyme rhythm for a monstrous figure. The officer, who has 'speed[ed] glum heroes up the line to death', will 'toddle' home safe and unscathed, his body intact. The word anchors a semantic field of safe, privileged movement that opposes the soldiers' 'line to death': the heroes march and die; the officer toddles and lives. The final dash before 'in bed' creates a savage, mocking pause: the officer will die not in the mud but in sheets, surrounded by comfort, the only thing he has ever known. Sassoon's anti-war rage, channelled through this satirical persona, reaches its peak in the word's dismissive triviality.
ex_en: When the war finally ended and the young were stone dead, the officer would toddle safely home to die peacefully in his own bed.
ex_zh: He had toddled through a war as old men do, and every step he took was a small, soft insult to the young legs that would never toddle again.

def_en: a piece of furniture for sleeping or resting; a place of death, especially of old age
def_zh: die in bed, safely home and die—in bed, old man's bed
quote_en: "And when the war is done and youth stone dead, / I'd toddle safely home and die—in bed." – The officer's death, in contrast to the soldiers', will be comfortable, natural, and in his own home (Base Details, 1917)
quote_zh: The final word 'bed' is the ultimate contrast to the trench, the graveyard, the mud. A bed is the site of sleep, safety, and domestic warmth, the precise opposite of the 'line' the heroes were sent to. The word is short, blunt, /b/ and /ɛd/ a soft landing, yet it hits like a hammer after the dash. Sassoon places it at the end of the poem, isolated by punctuation, so that the reader is left staring at this object of comfort that the dead will never know. The word belongs to a semantic field of softness and privilege that the poem has systematically constructed: 'best hotel', 'guzzling', 'toddle', and finally 'bed'. The officer's bed is the moral centre of the poem's anger: it is where the men who started the war will end it, while the young men who were sent to fight lie in unmarked graves. The rhyme with 'dead' (line 9) is a bitter, closed couplet: youth is stone dead, the officer dies in bed. No justice, no reckoning, just the indifferent passage of time.
ex_en: The officer would die in his own bed, soft and warm, while the youth he had sent to the front lay stone dead in a foreign field.
ex_zh: The bed he climbed into each night was a white field of surrender, and he sank into it like a stone thrown into a sea of feathers, grateful and guilty until the dark swallowed him whole.

def_en: a frightening dream; a terrifying experience or place
def_zh: nightmare ground, waking nightmare, return to the nightmare
quote_en: "Three weeks gone and the combatants gone / returning over the nightmare ground" – The speaker, a soldier, revisits the battlefield weeks after the fighting, finding the landscape still saturated with horror (Vergissmeinnicht, 1943)
quote_zh: Douglas opens with the compound ‘nightmare ground’, fusing psychological horror with physical terrain. A nightmare belongs to sleep, but here it has become earth underfoot, something the soldiers must walk across in waking life. The word’s /naɪt/ and /meər/ carry a dragging, heavy rhythm, the open vowels like a cry muffled by darkness. By pairing it with ‘ground’, Douglas insists that the battlefield is not merely dangerous but psychically cursed, a space that will haunt memory. This word establishes the poem’s governing tension: the encounter with the dead enemy soldier will take place not on a field of honour but in a landscape of bad dreams. The ‘nightmare’ is shared—the living and the dead both inhabit it. Douglas, killed in Normandy in 1944, wrote from direct armoured combat experience; his choice of ‘nightmare’ over ‘desolate’ or ‘silent’ reveals the war’s invasion of the unconscious.
ex_en: Three weeks after the battle, the soldiers returned across the nightmare ground where the fighting had taken place, and the horror still clung to the earth.
ex_zh: The nightmare was not the shell that had killed him, but the silence that followed, in which the only sound was the photograph of a smiling girl fluttering against his still-warm chest.

def_en: discovered; came upon, especially unexpectedly
def_zh: found the place, found the soldier, found his photograph
quote_en: "we found the place again, and found / the soldier sprawling in the sun." – Returning to the battlefield, the soldiers locate the spot and discover the enemy dead, lying exposed under the sun (Vergissmeinnicht, 1943)
quote_zh: Douglas repeats ‘found’ in a single line to mimic the double shock of recognition and discovery. The first ‘found’ locates the ground, the second locates the man; together they collapse the abstract idea of a battle site into the concrete reality of a corpse. The verb is simple and active, but its repetition slows the line, creating a ritualistic quality, as if the soldiers are performing a ceremony of retrieval. The /faʊnd/ diphthong opens the mouth and then closes it with a dental stop, a miniature movement of wonder and dread. The word belongs to a semantic field of recovery and exposure that runs through the poem: the gun is found, the photograph is found, the lover is found in death. Douglas, a commander of a tank, knew that returning to a battlefield meant encountering the enemy not as an abstraction but as a body, and ‘found’ is the precise, unadorned verb for that awful meeting.
ex_en: The soldiers returned to the battlefield, found the same spot, and there found the dead enemy soldier sprawled in the sunlight.
ex_zh: What he found in the sand was not a rifle but a hand that still seemed to be holding something, and when he bent down, he found it was a lock of hair tied with a blue ribbon.

def_en: casting a shadow over; looming over in a menacing or dominant way
def_zh: frowning barrel overshadowing, overshadowing the sun, fear overshadowing
quote_en: "The frowning barrel of his gun / overshad-owing. As we came on" – The dead soldier's gun still points outward, its barrel casting a metaphorical shadow over the scene (Vergissmeinnicht, 1943)
quote_zh: Douglas fractures ‘overshadowing’ across a line break—‘overshad- / owing’—to enact the very suspension it describes. The hyphen forces a pause, a hovering, as if the gun’s threat has been frozen in time. The word suggests both a literal shadow (the barrel blocking light) and a figurative menace that continues even after its owner is dead. The present participle keeps the action open and unfinished; the gun is still overshadowing, still threatening. This word belongs to a semantic field of threat and weaponry: ‘frowning barrel’, ‘hit my tank’, ‘entry of a demon’. The enemy soldier is no longer a combatant, but his weapon retains its agency, an inanimate object that lingers with intent. By laying the word across two lines, Douglas forces the reader’s eye into the same stutter of perception the soldiers experienced, the moment of seeing a weapon and not knowing if it will fire.
ex_en: The dead man's gun, still frowning, was overshadowing the scene, its barrel a dark line across the sun-warmed ground.
ex_zh: The memory of that day did not fade but grew, overshadowing every other summer of his life until even the sunniest afternoons felt like standing under a gun barrel.

def_en: an evil spirit or supernatural being; a destructive, malevolent force
def_zh: like a demon, demon entry, demon of war
quote_en: "that day, he hit my tank with one / like the entry of a demon." – The dead soldier, when alive and fighting, had struck the speaker's tank with a shell that felt as violent and invasive as a demon entering (Vergissmeinnicht, 1943)
quote_zh: Douglas’s simile ‘like the entry of a demon’ transforms the shell hit into a supernatural possession. The word ‘demon’ suggests a force that is not merely mechanical but malevolently alive, invading the armoured interior of the tank with evil intent. The /diː/ and /mən/ sounds are harsh and quick, the vowel long before the final unstressed syllable falls away like a curse. The ‘entry’ implies a violation of a sealed space, a wound that is both physical and spiritual. This word anchors a semantic field of the infernal and violent that the poem will later counterpoint with the tenderness of the photograph. The dead soldier, who is now a sprawling corpse, was a demonic attacker in the clash of battle; the poem’s argument is that both identities—lover and killer, demon and boy—inhabit the same body. Douglas, educated at Oxford and steeped in Renaissance poetry, draws on the old sense of ‘demon’ as a spirit of the air, something that enters from another world.
ex_en: The enemy shell had hit his tank with such force that it felt like a demon entering through the armour, an alien and malevolent presence.
ex_zh: The demon he had met that day was not the soldier who fired the shot but the war that had put the gun in his hand and the photograph in his pocket.

def_en: the phrase 'forget-me-not'; a plea in a loved one's handwriting
def_zh: Steffi Vergissmeinnicht, copybook Vergissmeinnicht, written Vergissmeinnicht
quote_en: "who has put: Steffi. Vergißmeinnicht / in a copybook gothic script." – The dead soldier carries a photograph inscribed by a girl named Steffi, with the German plea 'forget me not' written in her careful handwriting (Vergissmeinnicht, 1943)
quote_zh: The German word ‘Vergissmeinnicht’ is the poem’s emotional core, a word that crosses languages and allegiances to lodge itself in the reader’s mind. It is both a proper inscription and a universal plea: the dead man, who is an enemy, was loved by a girl who does not want to be forgotten. Douglas does not translate the word, preserving its foreignness, forcing the English reader to encounter it as the soldier did—as alien text that suddenly becomes legible as human feeling. The word’s soft fricatives and the delicate /maɪ̯ nɪçt/ mimic the intimacy of a whisper. It belongs to a semantic field of writing and memory: ‘copybook gothic script’, ‘put: Steffi’, ‘photograph’, ‘we see’. The inscription transforms the corpse from a military target into a lover, a son, a person. The flower ‘forget-me-not’, which the word also names, is never mentioned but hovers behind the text, a hidden symbol of fidelity that war has crushed into the dust of the gunpit.
ex_en: Among his clothes, the dead soldier carried a photograph inscribed by a girl named Steffi, who had written 'Vergissmeinnicht'—forget me not—in her neat schoolgirl script.
ex_zh: He could not pronounce the word, but he knew what it meant: it meant a girl somewhere would water a small blue flower every evening and wait for a knock that would never sound.

def_en: the amount paid or required in exchange for something; the cost of an action or consequence
def_zh: soldier's price, pay the price, price of guilt
quote_en: "abased, and seeming to have paid / a soldier's price for a civilian's guilt," – The dead soldier appears humbled, as if he has atoned with his life for a guilt that belongs not to him but to the wider world (Vergissmeinnicht, 1943)
quote_zh: Douglas uses ‘price’ to reframe the soldier’s death as a transaction he did not authorise. The metaphor ‘paid a soldier's price’ invokes a marketplace of sacrifice, where the young man’s life is the currency and ‘a civilian's guilt’ is the debt being settled. The word’s sharp /pr/ and hissing /aɪs/ sound almost commercial, a cold accounting. The adjective ‘abased’ suggests the soldier is humiliated in death, his sprawled body an object of payment for something he did not commit. The phrase is bitter and ironic: the guilt belongs to politicians, propagandists, the home front, but the cheque is cashed in young flesh. ‘Price’ anchors a semantic field of exchange and injustice that runs beneath the poem: the enemy has paid dearly, but what has been bought? Douglas suggests that the enemy is not a monster but a fellow victim, and the price he has paid is shared by all the war’s dead.
ex_en: The dead soldier seemed to have paid with his life—a soldier's price—for a guilt that belonged not to him but to the civilians who had started the war.
ex_zh: The price of the photograph was not the few coins he had paid the camera-man, but the life that had ended beside it, and the girl who would never know that her handwriting had become a receipt.

def_en: injury, pain, or distress, especially emotional
def_zh: mortal hurt, love's hurt, hurt done
quote_en: "And death who had the soldier singled / has done the lover mortal hurt." – Death, having killed the soldier, has also dealt a fatal wound to the lover within him, the man who loved Steffi (Vergissmeinnicht, 1943)
quote_zh: Douglas closes the poem on ‘hurt’, a word that collapses physical injury and emotional grief into a single, plain noun. ‘Mortal hurt’ is a wound that kills, but the victim here is not the soldier (already dead) but the lover, the part of him that held and cherished the photograph. The word’s /h/ is a breath, and /ɜːrt/ an earthy, guttural sound, as if the pain is being pushed out. The enjambment from ‘has done the lover’ to ‘mortal hurt’ suspends the sentence, forcing the reader to wait for the full impact. ‘Hurt’ is simpler than ‘grief’ or ‘wound’; it is the language of a child or a soldier stripped of eloquence. The poem’s final assertion—that death has ‘singled’ the soldier but wounded the lover—is an act of imaginative empathy that crosses enemy lines. Douglas, a poet who would himself die in combat, writes a line that serves as an epitaph for all the lovers whose photographs are found in the pockets of the dead.
ex_en: Death may have singled out the soldier for its aim, but it has dealt the lover within him a mortal hurt that no bullet can reach.
ex_zh: The hurt she felt when the telegram came was a mortal wound, and she carried it for seventy years, watering the forget-me-nots with a hand that never forgot.

def_en: having abundant possessions; fertile, magnificent; also, morally or spiritually wealthy
def_zh: rich Dead, rich gifts, rich harvest
quote_en: "Blow out, you bugles, over the rich Dead!" – The speaker calls upon bugles to sound a fanfare over the war dead, who are described as abundantly wealthy in a spiritual sense (The Dead, 1914)
quote_zh: Brooke applies ‘rich’ to the dead, reversing the expected meaning of loss. Financially, most of these young soldiers owned little, but their sacrifice has endowed them with a wealth beyond gold. The adjective instantly elevates them above pity, placing them in a category of moral aristocracy. The /rɪtʃ/ sound is crisp and affirmative, ending with a decisive affricate. It belongs to a semantic field of treasure and royalty that runs through the sonnet: ‘gold’, ‘royal wage’, ‘king’, ‘heritage’. By opening with this paradox, Brooke announces that the poem will not be an elegy of grief but a celebration of gain. The dead are not beggared by war but enriched, their value multiplied in the national ledger of honour. The word also echoes the liturgical ‘rich in mercy’, giving the war a sacramental frame.
ex_en: The dead soldiers are rich, not with money, but with the immense spiritual value their sacrifice has given to the nation.
ex_zh: The richest man at the memorial service wore no coat but lay under a white stone, his pockets filled only with a photograph and a letter that smelled of lavender.

def_en: a precious yellow metal; symbol of wealth, purity, and enduring value
def_zh: rarer gifts than gold, worth gold, gold of youth
quote_en: "But, dying, has made us rarer gifts than gold." – The speaker asserts that the deaths of soldiers have given the living gifts more precious than any material wealth (The Dead, 1914)
quote_zh: Brooke uses ‘gold’ as the ultimate measure of earthly value, only to declare it inferior to the soldiers’ sacrifice. Gold is durable and beautiful, but ‘rarer gifts’ exceed it: honour, holiness, love, and pain. The long vowel /oʊ/ lends the word a warm, glowing sonority, while the alliteration with ‘gifts’ and ‘gave’ binds giving and gold. The word anchors a semantic field of treasure and transcendent worth: ‘gold’, ‘gifts’, ‘wage’, ‘heritage’. By measuring the dead against gold, Brooke adopts the language of commerce to spiritualize it; the transaction of war is not a loss but a heavenly investment. The idea that death ‘makes’ gifts is a defiant act of creation, turning corpses into a moral currency that will outlast any coin. In the context of 1914, before the full scale of slaughter became known, the line reads as a patriotic assertion of meaning.
ex_en: The dead soldiers’ sacrifice produced gifts rarer than gold, treasures of honour and holiness that the nation had long lacked.
ex_zh: He had traded all the gold of his youth—unspent years, undrunk wine, unloved lips—and in return received a name carved in stone that strangers touched on Remembrance Day.

def_en: an alcoholic drink made from fermented grapes; symbol of celebration, blood, or sacrifice
def_zh: sweet wine, wine of youth, pour out wine
quote_en: "These laid the world away; poured out the red / Sweet wine of youth;" – The dead soldiers have given up their young lives, described as a precious red wine poured freely onto the ground (The Dead, 1914)
quote_zh: Brooke’s metaphor of the ‘red sweet wine of youth’ transforms slaughter into libation, an offering to the gods. Wine is celebratory, sacred, and intoxicating; the soldiers have poured away their vitality as if making a ritual gift. The adjective ‘sweet’ heightens the sense of youth’s deliciousness, lost before it could be fully savoured. The /waɪn/ sound is long and fading, like a note held on a horn. The word belongs to a semantic field of liquid sacrifice that includes ‘poured out’, ‘red’, ‘blood’ (unspoken but present). Brooke draws on the Christian Eucharist, where wine becomes blood, to sanctify the soldiers’ deaths without explicitly mentioning the wounds. This deliberate aestheticization of death is both the poem’s power and its later controversy: the wine conceals the gore, but it also offers a vision of meaning that the bereaved needed to hear in 1914.
ex_en: The soldiers poured out the sweet red wine of their youth, giving up every future joy as a willing sacrifice.
ex_zh: The wine they poured was not drunk but soaked into a foreign field, and decades later the grapes that grew there were the colour of a boy’s first kiss.

def_en: the ability to live forever; enduring fame; eternal life in memory or spirit
def_zh: gave their immortality, won immortality, immortality of youth
quote_en: "and those who would have been, / Their sons, they gave, their immortality." – The dead soldiers, by dying childless, have surrendered not only their lives but the continuation of their bloodline, their mortal future (The Dead, 1914)
quote_zh: The word ‘immortality’ is the poem’s most ambitious claim, yet here it is ambiguous: the soldiers gave it away. They will have no sons, no living descendants; their biological immortality is forfeit. But the poem implicitly offers a different immortality—in memory, in national legend, in the inheritance of the living. The /ɪmɔːrˈtælɪti/ sound rolls like a solemn bell, the five syllables stretching into a promise. The word crowns the sonnet’s first movement, the octave, with a paradox: by dying they achieve a different kind of permanence. Brooke’s own death in 1915, shortly after writing these lines, sealed the poem’s meaning; he became one of the rich dead he had celebrated. The word belongs to a semantic field of time and legacy: ‘years’, ‘age’, ‘sons’, ‘heritage’. It suggests that the dead have not vanished but been translated into a condition beyond decay.
ex_en: The young soldiers gave up their chance at earthly immortality—the sons they would never father—but in doing so gained another kind, living forever in the nation’s grateful memory.
ex_zh: Immortality, it turned out, was not a golden throne but a name printed in a list beneath a wet newspaper on a November morning, read by a mother who could still feel the weight of an infant in her arms.

def_en: the state of being holy; sacred character; moral and spiritual perfection
def_zh: brought us Holiness, Holiness lacked, gift of Holiness
quote_en: "They brought us, for our dearth, / Holiness, lacked so long, and Love, and Pain." – The soldiers have restored holiness to a spiritually impoverished world, alongside love and pain (The Dead, 1914)
quote_zh: Brooke capitalises ‘Holiness’ to personify it as a returning virtue, a sacred quality that had been absent from modern, materialistic life. The word’s /hoʊ/ is aspirated and reverent, and the /lɪnəs/ softens into a hushed reverence. It belongs to a semantic field of sacred gifts alongside ‘Love’ and ‘Pain’, a trinity of spiritual renewal won by the dead. The enjambment from ‘dearth’ to ‘Holiness’ enacts the filling of a void; the empty first line is completed by the sacred presence. Brooke draws on a widespread sentiment in 1914 that war would purify a decadent civilisation, a belief later shattered by the trenches. Yet within the sonnet’s closed logic, ‘Holiness’ is the highest gift: not safety, not victory, but the moral cleansing of a nation willing to sacrifice its best. The word suggests that the soldiers have become not just heroes but saints, their blood a consecration.
ex_en: The dead soldiers, through their sacrifice, brought back to a spiritually starved world the Holiness it had long lacked.
ex_zh: Holiness was not a white robe but a mud-caked tunic, and it walked not in cathedrals but in the silence that fell after the last bugle note died away.

def_en: high respect; adherence to what is right; a personified virtue; a public mark of esteem
def_zh: Honour has come back, king Honour, bring Honour back
quote_en: "Honour has come back, as a king, to earth, / And paid his subjects with a royal wage;" – The speaker personifies Honour as a returning monarch who rewards the nation with the noble example of the dead (The Dead, 1914)
quote_zh: Brooke personifies ‘Honour’ as a king returning to a world that had forgotten him. The capital letter, the archaic spelling, and the simile ‘as a king’ elevate the concept into a living presence. The /ɒnər/ sound is round and dignified, almost a bow. The word anchors a semantic field of royalty and reward that structures the sestet: ‘king’, ‘subjects’, ‘royal wage’, ‘Nobleness’, ‘heritage’. The dead have paid the price, but Honour returns to claim them as his own. This is a reversal of the usual lament: instead of honour being owed to the dead, the dead have restored honour to the living. Brooke draws on the chivalric code to argue that the war has revived moral qualities that peace had eroded. The phrase ‘paid his subjects’ suggests that even Honour owes a debt, and the dead are its currency. The word is placed at the start of line 11, a strong initial beat that announces the poem’s turn towards resolution.
ex_en: Honour, personified as a returning king, has come back to the earth and paid the nation the wage of a noble example.
ex_zh: Honour was a cold comfort when the telegram came, but it was the only blanket she had, and she wrapped herself in it until the shivering stopped.

def_en: the quality of being noble in character, mind, or bearing; distinction
def_zh: Nobleness walks, bring back Nobleness, forgotten Nobleness
quote_en: "And Nobleness walks in our ways again; / And we have come into our heritage." – The poem closes with the figure of Nobleness, restored to everyday life by the soldiers’ sacrifice (The Dead, 1914)
quote_zh: The final personified virtue is ‘Nobleness’, a word that echoes the ‘rich Dead’ and the ‘royal wage’ to complete the poem’s argument of regained moral greatness. The /noʊbəlnəs/ sound is spacious and measured, a walking rhythm that enacts the ‘walks in our ways’. The word belongs to a semantic field of aristocratic virtue that Brooke deliberately democratises: it is not inherited rank but a quality of spirit now available to all. The verb ‘walks’ is domestic and unassuming, suggesting that nobility is not a distant ideal but a presence in the street, in the homes of the bereaved. The final couplet pairs ‘Nobleness’ with ‘heritage’, implying that the dead have bequeathed a moral legacy. Brooke’s sonnet thus ends not in grief but in inheritance, the living accepting the gift and the responsibility. Written months before the war’s true scale unravelled, the poem captures a moment when it was possible to believe that death could be transfigured into national redemption.
ex_en: Nobleness now walks through our daily lives again, a restored quality we have inherited from the dead.
ex_zh: Nobleness, she learned, was not a statue on a horse but the way her father, who had seen too much, still held the door open for strangers and never spoke of what he had lost.

def_en: showing no mercy or pity; cruel and unrelenting
def_zh: merciless winds, merciless cold, merciless iced
quote_en: "Our brains ache, in the merciless iced east winds that knife us ..." – The speaker, a soldier, describes the freezing wind cutting through the men's skulls with pitiless force (Exposure, 1917)
quote_zh: Owen personifies the wind as ‘merciless’, a word that transforms weather into an active, malevolent enemy. Unlike ‘cold’ or ‘biting’, ‘merciless’ implies conscious cruelty, as if the elements have chosen to inflict suffering without reprieve. The word belongs to a semantic field of assault and violence—‘knife’, ‘ache’, ‘mad gusts’—that collapses the distinction between the German line and the winter air. Phonetically, the sibilant /s/ slices through the middle of the word, mimicking the blade of the wind. Owen, writing from the frozen trenches of January 1917, knew that exposure killed as surely as shells. The adjective also carries a theological weight: a merciless world implies an absent or indifferent God, a theme the poem will develop in its final stanzas. By placing ‘merciless’ before ‘iced east winds’, Owen makes the weather personal, an executioner.
ex_en: The soldiers' minds throbbed with pain in the merciless, ice-edged wind that cut into them without pity.
ex_zh: The merciless thing was not the cold itself but the way it crept into memory, freezing not just the present but every warm recollection until all that remained was a white, howling blank.

def_en: to stab or cut with a sharp blade; used metaphorically for wind that slices
def_zh: winds that knife, knife us, knifing wind
quote_en: "Our brains ache, in the merciless iced east winds that knife us ..." – The wind is not merely cold but cuts like a weapon, stabbing at the soldiers' exposed bodies (Exposure, 1917)
quote_zh: Owen’s choice of ‘knife’ as a verb transforms the wind from a natural phenomenon into a deliberate weapon. The word is short, monosyllabic, and violent; its hard /k/ and /f/ evoke a sharp stab. By placing it at the line break, Owen forces a momentary pause that mimics the intake of breath before a wound. The metaphorical knife belongs to a semantic field of bodily assault that the poem sustains: ‘ache’, ‘twitching agonies’, ‘bullets streak’, ‘shrivelling’. The soldiers are not merely weathering the cold; they are being stabbed by it. This verb also prepares for the later, more explicit weaponry of ‘bullets’ and ‘shells’, suggesting that nature itself has joined the war on the side of death. Owen, who endured the winter of 1916–17 in the open, knew that the wind was as much an enemy as the Bosche.
ex_en: The wind did not blow; it knifed them, a blade of ice that sliced through their thin coats and into their skulls.
ex_zh: The knife of memory was not the shell blast but the soft whisper of the wind on a spring afternoon that sounded exactly like the day before he made his mother cry.

def_en: hanging or bending downwards limply; sagging from exhaustion or despair
def_zh: drooping flares, low drooping, drooping heads
quote_en: "Low drooping flares confuse our memory of the salient ..." – The signal flares hang low in the sky, their feeble light blurring the soldiers' recollection of the battlefield layout (Exposure, 1917)
quote_zh: Owen selects ‘drooping’ to describe the flares, a word that drains them of any military sharpness or heroic brightness. Flares should soar and illuminate; these ‘droop’ like dying flowers or exhausted heads. The /druːp/ syllable is drawn and heavy, the /ɪŋ/ suffix suggesting an ongoing, incomplete action. The word belongs to a semantic field of weariness and decline—‘wearied’, ‘drowse’, ‘shivering’, ‘dying’—that paints the entire trench as a body sinking into the mud. The drooping flares also fail in their purpose: instead of clarifying the salient, they confuse memory, just as the soldiers' own minds are failing under the strain. Owen, who suffered shell-shock, knew that perception itself could droop, that the world could lose its edges. The adjective gives the light a human posture of defeat.
ex_en: The signal flares hung low and drooping in the sky, their weak glow doing nothing but jumbling the soldiers' memories of the front line.
ex_zh: The drooping of his heart was imperceptible at first, like a petal bending under rain, until one day he realised he could no longer lift his eyes to the horizon.

def_en: mentally ill; insane; also, frantically wild and uncontrolled
def_zh: mad gusts, mad winds, mad with cold
quote_en: "Watching, we hear the mad gusts tugging on the wire." – The wind gusts are described as insane, wild, and out of control as they jerk at the barbed wire (Exposure, 1917)
quote_zh: Owen uses ‘mad’ to personify the wind, lending it a psychological disorder. The word suggests both fury and insanity, as if nature has lost its reason. The /mæd/ syllable is blunt and unhinged, the vowel flattened by exhaustion. It belongs to a semantic field of mental fragmentation that includes ‘confuse’, ‘memory’, ‘worried by silence’, ‘snow-dazed’, and later ‘dying’. The ‘mad gusts’ are the chaotic outward expression of an inward disintegration; the soldiers, too, feel their hold on sanity slipping. The verb ‘tugging’ emphasises a repetitive, compulsive action, like a madman pulling at restraints. Owen draws on his own experience of seeing men break down in the trenches, their minds as ravaged as their bodies. The wire, a symbol of the war’s entrapment, is being jerked by forces beyond reason.
ex_en: The soldiers listened to the mad gusts of wind wrenching at the barbed wire, a frantic, insane sound in the darkness.
ex_zh: The mad part was not the shrieking of the wind but the silence that followed, which seemed to have more screaming in it than any gale.

def_en: shakes or trembles convulsively, especially from fear, cold, or revulsion
def_zh: air shudders, earth shudders, body shudders
quote_en: "Less deadly than the air that shudders black with snow," – The air itself shivers and trembles, filled with dark, swirling snow (Exposure, 1917)
quote_zh: Owen deploys ‘shudders’ to make the air a living, terrified body. The verb typically describes a human reaction to horror or cold; applied to the atmosphere, it suggests a world convulsed by the war. The /ʃ/ sound is shivering, the /ʌdər/ a rapid tremor, and the sibilant ending mimics snow hissing. The word belongs to a semantic field of bodily reaction that runs through the poem: ‘ache’, ‘cringe’, ‘shrivelling’, ‘shaking’. The air ‘shudders black with snow’, a colour inversion that turns whiteness into a form of darkness, a blizzard that is also a blackout. Owen emphasises that this shuddering air is ‘less deadly’ than the bullets, but it is deadly enough: men freeze to death in it. The verb also anticipates the later shudder of the dying men, creating a spectral continuity between the weather and the flesh.
ex_en: The air shuddered, filled with dark, swirling snow, as if the sky itself were trembling with cold and fear.
ex_zh: The shudder that ran through her shoulders was not from the November wind but from the memory of reading the words ‘died of exposure’ and realising it meant he had simply been left in the cold until breathing stopped.

def_en: to be half-asleep; to doze fitfully, especially when one should be alert
def_zh: we drowse, drowse snow-dazed, drowse and dream
quote_en: "Deep into grassier ditches. So we drowse, sun-dozed," – The soldiers, numbed by cold and exhaustion, slip into a half-sleeping state in the ditches (Exposure, 1917)
quote_zh: Owen’s ‘drowse’ captures the dangerous, hypnotic borderland between waking and freezing to death. The verb is soft and languid, its /draʊz/ sound a slow, heavy drift. Yet drowsing in a trench is lethal; it is the prelude to exposure, the body surrendering to the cold. The word belongs to a semantic field of fading consciousness: ‘drowse’, ‘sun-dozed’, ‘forgotten dreams’, ‘stare, snow-dazed’. Owen contrasts this with the earlier hyper-vigilance of ‘watching’ and ‘worried by silence’, showing the arc from alertness to stupor. The compound ‘sun-dozed’ is bitterly ironic: there is no sun, only the memory of it, and the men are ‘dozed’ not by warmth but by the sedative of hypothermia. Owen, having watched men freeze, knew that the desire to sleep was a symptom of death approaching.
ex_en: The soldiers drowsed in the frozen ditches, their bodies so numbed that consciousness was slipping into a dangerous, snow-dazed near-sleep.
ex_zh: He drowse still, in the photograph, or so she imagined, his eyes half-closed not from fatigue but from the flash powder of the studio, and she would wait forever for him to open them fully.

def_en: made glossy or shining; glazed over, especially with a deceptive appearance
def_zh: glozed with jewels, fires glozed, glozed crust
quote_en: "Slowly our ghosts drag home: glimpsing the sunk fires glozed / With crusted dark-red jewels;" – The dying soldiers imagine their spirits returning home to see domestic fires banked and glowing with a glossy, jewel-like crust (Exposure, 1917)
quote_zh: Owen reaches for the rare, archaic ‘glozed’ to create a vision of homely warmth that is simultaneously rich and eerie. The word suggests a surface brightness that conceals, a deceptive gloss. The home fires are ‘glozed’ with ‘crusted dark-red jewels’, an image that transforms the glowing embers into treasure, but also into something sealed off and unattainable. The /ɡloʊ/ echoes ‘glow’, but the hard /zd/ ending is rough and final, suggesting the gloss is a skin over death. The word stands at the threshold of a semantic field of domestic comfort that the soldiers can only glimpse: ‘fires’, ‘crickets’, ‘mice’, ‘shutters’, ‘doors’. Yet all these warm things are ‘closed’ to them; the gloze is a barrier, not an invitation. Owen, who wrote often of the unreachable home, makes the word a miniature of the whole poem’s argument: the soldiers catch a vision of what they are dying to protect, but they cannot reach it.
ex_en: The ghosts of the soldiers imagined they saw the banked home fires glozed with a crust of glowing, dark-red jewels.
ex_zh: The window was glozed with frost, and behind the ice-flowers he thought he could see the outline of a hearth, but his hand could not wipe the cold away.

def_en: to close or attach firmly; to fix securely; here, of frost seizing the men
def_zh: fasten on, fasten us, frost will fasten
quote_en: "To-night, His frost will fasten on this mud and us," – The frost, attributed to God, will grip both the ground and the soldiers in its freezing hold (Exposure, 1917)
quote_zh: Owen uses ‘fasten’ to render frost as a grip, a locking mechanism that will fix the men to the earth. The verb suggests an intentional, almost mechanical action; the frost does not merely form—it fastens, latching onto mud and flesh with equal indifference. The /fæs/ and /ən/ sounds are sharp and then muffled, the /s/ hissing like ice forming. The capitalised ‘His’ refers to God, making the frost a divine act, an embrace that kills. The word belongs to a semantic field of enclosure and death: ‘fasten’, ‘shrivelling’, ‘puckering’, ‘burying-party’. The double object—‘this mud and us’—equates the soldiers with the ground they lie in, as if they are already part of the landscape. Owen, who had lost men to the cold, knew that frost fixed corpses in grotesque postures, fastening them to the trench floor. The verb is a quiet, terrifying statement of the poem’s final loss of faith: God’s love is not warming; it fastens.
ex_en: Tonight, the frost will fasten itself onto the mud and onto the soldiers, clamping them in a frozen grip.
ex_zh: The frost that fastened on his heart the morning the letter came did not melt, not even when the sun of spring returned and all the other ice had wept itself away.

def_en: moving at a speed faster than a walk; fleeing or charging
def_zh: suddenly running, running raw, running in khaki
quote_en: "Suddenly he awoke and was running – raw / In raw-seamed hot khaki, his sweat heavy," – A soldier, suddenly alert, is charging across a field in his rough uniform (Bayonet Charge, 1957)
quote_zh: Hughes opens mid-action with ‘running’, a present participle that thrusts the reader into the soldier’s panicked motion. The word’s /rʌnɪŋ/ sound is breathless, the double /n/ creating a continuous, stumbling rhythm. It belongs to a semantic field of frantic movement—‘stumbling’, ‘lugged’, ‘plunged’—that defines the soldier’s physical experience. The adverb ‘suddenly’ before the verb stresses the abrupt transition from passivity (sleep or stupor) to terrified action. Hughes, writing decades after the war, imagines the soldier not as a hero but as a body reacting instinctively. The repetition of ‘raw’ intensifies the sense of exposed, chafed flesh, and ‘running’ becomes less a tactical charge than a flight from an unknowable threat. The word sets the poem’s breakneck pace and its central question: what is he running toward, and what is he running from?
ex_en: The soldier suddenly woke and was running across the clodded field, his body raw and sweating inside the stiff khaki uniform.
ex_zh: He had been running for seventy years, through fields that turned into suburban streets and back again, never reaching the green hedge, never stopping.

def_en: (of skin) red and painful from exposure or friction; emotionally exposed; uncooked, brutal
def_zh: raw khaki, raw-seamed, running raw
quote_en: "Suddenly he awoke and was running – raw / In raw-seamed hot khaki, his sweat heavy," – The soldier is both physically chafed and emotionally exposed, his uniform seeming to scrape him raw (Bayonet Charge, 1957)
quote_zh: Hughes repeats ‘raw’ to hammer home the brutal exposure of the soldier’s condition. The word carries multiple meanings: the uniform seams are physically raw against the skin, the soldier’s nerves are raw with fear, and the whole experience is raw—unprocessed, unheroic, immediate. The /rɔː/ vowel is drawn out, almost a howl. It anchors a semantic field of bodily pain and vulnerability: ‘sweat heavy’, ‘numb’, ‘molten iron’, ‘terror’s touchy dynamite’. The enjambment of ‘raw / In raw-seamed’ forces the reader to feel the repetition as a physical blow. Hughes, influenced by the animal poetry he also wrote, presents the soldier as a creature of sensation, stripped of ideology. The rawness is both the chafing of the uniform and the stripping away of patriotic illusion.
ex_en: The soldier's skin was raw, rubbed by the rough seams of his uniform, and his whole being was raw with unthinking terror.
ex_zh: The memory was still raw, sixty years on, a wound that had never learned to scar, only to open again whenever a car backfired or a child screamed in play.

def_en: lumps of earth or clay, often hard and uneven
def_zh: stumbling across clods, field of clods, heavy clods
quote_en: "Stumbling across a field of clods towards a green hedge / That dazzled with rifle fire," – The soldier trips over hard lumps of earth as he runs toward the hedge that spits bullets (Bayonet Charge, 1957)
quote_zh: Hughes uses ‘clods’ to ground the poem in a physical, rural landscape that has been militarised. A clod is a lump of ploughed earth, normally associated with farming and peace, but here it becomes an obstacle in a killing field. The word’s /klɒdz/ sound is heavy and blunt, mimicking the thud of a boot catching on earth. It belongs to a semantic field of terrain and obstruction: ‘field’, ‘furrows’, ‘green hedge’, ‘shot-slashed’. The soldier stumbles across the clods, his body out of sync with the ground, underscoring his helplessness. The detail is precise: the charge is not on a smooth parade ground but on rough, broken farmland. The clods also prefigure the hare that will be thrown up later, both creatures of the field disrupted by mechanised violence.
ex_en: The soldier stumbled across a field of hard clods, each lump of earth threatening to trip him as he ran toward the shooting hedge.
ex_zh: The clods of earth that he kicked up as he ran were the last things his father ever touched; they sent them home in a small bag, and the soil was not even from his own country.

def_en: deprived of sensation; unable to feel or respond
def_zh: numb as a smashed arm, numb with shock, numb rifle
quote_en: "He lugged a rifle numb as a smashed arm;" – The soldier carries his rifle, but it feels dead, alien, and unfeeling, like an arm that has been shattered (Bayonet Charge, 1957)
quote_zh: Hughes’s simile ‘numb as a smashed arm’ equates the soldier’s weapon with a destroyed limb. The word ‘numb’ suggests both physical insensitivity and emotional deadening; the rifle is not an extension of the soldier’s will but a dead weight. The /nʌm/ sound is closed and muffled, the lips closing over the final /m/ as if refusing sensation. The simile transforms the rifle from a tool of agency into a wound, a part of the body that has been rendered useless. The word belongs to a semantic field of bodily damage and disconnection: ‘smashed’, ‘sweat heavy’, ‘molten iron’, ‘terror’. The soldier ‘lugs’ the rifle—it is a burden, not an instrument. Hughes suggests that the soldier’s relationship to the war is one of passive suffering, not active combat. He is not shooting; he is carrying, stumbling, and being shot at.
ex_en: The soldier lugged his rifle like a numb, smashed arm, a weight that could no longer feel or respond to his will.
ex_zh: The numbness did not arrive with the bullet but with the silence after; a stillness that crept up his fingers and into his chest, where it stayed long after the wound had healed.

def_en: feeling or expressing devotion to one's country
def_zh: patriotic tear, patriotic fervour, patriotic reason
quote_en: "The patriotic tear that had brimmed in his eye / Sweating like molten iron from the centre of his chest, –" – The soldier once felt a tear of patriotism, but now that emotion is converted into something heavy, hot, and metallic (Bayonet Charge, 1957)
quote_zh: Hughes uses ‘patriotic’ to name the ideology that sent the soldier into the charge, only to show it dissolving into a hot, painful secretion. The word is carefully placed at the end of a line, granting it a momentary dignity before the next line violates it. The /pætriˈɒtɪk/ sound is multisyllabic and formal, the register of speeches and slogans, contrasting with the raw physicality surrounding it. It belongs to a semantic field of abstract ideals that the poem systematically destroys: ‘King, honour, human dignity, etcetera’. The tear is ‘patriotic’, but it sweats out of the chest ‘like molten iron’, transforming a noble emotion into a scalding exudation. Hughes suggests that patriotism is not a sustaining force in combat but a burden that melts under pressure, leaving only pain and bewilderment. The body rejects the idea as it would a foreign substance.
ex_en: The tear of patriotism that once welled in his eye was now sweating out of his chest as heavy, burning iron.
ex_zh: The patriotic song still played in the village hall, but the notes were just sounds now, words that had leaked their meaning into the mud of a field he could not name.

def_en: a mechanism of interlocking gears; something that operates with mechanical regularity
def_zh: cold clockwork, clockwork of the stars, clockwork nations
quote_en: "In what cold clockwork of the stars and the nations / Was he the hand pointing that second?" – The soldier suddenly becomes aware of the vast, mechanical forces that have placed him at this specific moment of danger (Bayonet Charge, 1957)
quote_zh: Hughes’s ‘clockwork’ transforms the cosmos and the political order into an intricate, indifferent machine. The word suggests precision without intention, a universe that ticks without caring. The /klɒk/ and /wɜːrk/ sounds are hard and mechanical, the syllables clicking like gears. It belongs to a semantic field of machinery and determinism: ‘cold’, ‘stars’, ‘nations’, ‘hand pointing’. The soldier, who is nothing but a body in motion, is suddenly aware of himself as a tiny cog. The question ‘Was he the hand pointing that second?’ is both literal (the second hand of a clock) and metaphorical (an agent directed by fate). Hughes channels the bewildered moment when a soldier realises he is disposable, a moving part in a system too vast to comprehend. The coldness of the clockwork emphasises the absence of meaning or mercy.
ex_en: The soldier wondered, in a flash of bewildered clarity, what cold, indifferent clockwork of the stars and the warring nations had made him the hand pointing at this very second.
ex_zh: The clockwork of his life had been wound by a stranger, and he spent the rest of his years trying to find the key, not to stop the ticking, but to understand whose hand had turned it.

def_en: a fast-running, long-eared mammal, related to the rabbit; a symbol of wild innocence
def_zh: yellow hare, hare rolled like a flame, threshing hare
quote_en: "Threw up a yellow hare that rolled like a flame / And crawled in a threshing circle, its mouth wide / Open silent, its eyes standing out." – A hare, disturbed by gunfire, is thrown into the air and dies in a thrashing, silent agony (Bayonet Charge, 1957)
quote_zh: Hughes introduces the ‘hare’ as a figure of pure, innocent suffering, a wild animal caught in the machinery of human war. The hare is ‘yellow’, a colour that suggests both fear and a sickly death-pallor, while the simile ‘rolled like a flame’ gives the dying animal a strange, momentary beauty. The /heər/ sound is light and breathy, its softness contrasting with the violence of ‘threw up’ and ‘threshing’. The word belongs to a semantic field of natural life invaded by war: ‘field’, ‘clods’, ‘green hedge’, and now ‘hare’. The hare’s silent scream—‘its mouth wide / Open silent, its eyes standing out’—mirrors the soldier’s own voiceless terror. Hughes, a poet who wrote frequently about animals, presents the hare as a kind of double for the soldier: both are running, both are terrified, both are crushed by forces they cannot understand. The hare’s death is a brief, brutal parable of the charge itself.
ex_en: A yellow hare was thrown up by the gunfire, rolling in the air like a flame and then crawling in a threshing circle, its mouth wide open in a silent scream.
ex_zh: The hare he had seen dying in the field never left him; it became the shape of his own silence, curled in the corner of every photograph, its mouth open but giving no testimony.

def_en: easily upset or offended; liable to explode or ignite
def_zh: touchy dynamite, touchy terror, touchy nerves
quote_en: "His terror's touchy dynamite." – The soldier's fear has become a highly sensitive explosive, ready to detonate at the slightest contact (Bayonet Charge, 1957)
quote_zh: Hughes closes the poem with ‘touchy’, an adjective that redefines terror as a volatile, hair-trigger explosive. The word’s /tʌtʃi/ sound is sharp and twitchy, the /tʃ/ a sudden jolt. It belongs to a semantic field of explosion and instability: ‘dynamite’, ‘molten iron’, ‘blue crackling air’, ‘shot-slashed’. The personification of terror as ‘touchy dynamite’ suggests that the soldier’s psyche has been replaced by a weapon; he is no longer a man feeling fear but an unstable bomb. The word ‘touchy’ is informal, almost petulant, which undercuts any heroic register. The soldier is not brave or strong; he is a bundle of raw nerves that could go off at any moment. Hughes thus concludes that the effect of the charge is not triumph but a permanent state of barely controlled explosion, a condition that will outlast the war itself.
ex_en: The soldier’s terror was touchy dynamite, liable to explode at the slightest touch, a hair-trigger of fear.
ex_zh: The old man was touchy, everyone said so, but only his wife knew that the thing he flinched from was not the slamming door but the silence that followed, which always sounded like the moment before the shell landed.Proceeding with the fifteenth poem: **‘Remains’** by Simon Armitage.

def_en: a person who steals goods, especially during a war or riot
def_zh: tackle looters, kill a looter, looter raiding
quote_en: "On another occasion, we got sent out / to tackle looters raiding a bank." – The speaker, a soldier, recounts being deployed to stop civilians stealing from a bank during a conflict (Remains, 2008)
quote_zh: Armitage opens with the word ‘looter’, immediately framing the poem’s moral ambiguity. A looter is not a combatant but a civilian criminal, possibly desperate, possibly opportunistic. The word’s /luːt/ sound is drawn out, almost plaintive, and its placement at the end of the line gives it a weight of uncertainty. It belongs to a semantic field of crime and disorder—‘raiding’, ‘armed’, ‘legs it’—that contrasts with the military language of ‘sent out’ and ‘open fire’. By calling the victim a ‘looter’, the speaker attempts to justify the killing, yet the qualification ‘probably armed, possibly not’ immediately undermines that justification. Armitage, drawing on interviews with veterans of the Iraq War, shows how the label ‘looter’ is both a legal category and a psychological defence, a way of processing the act of killing a possibly unarmed man.
ex_en: The soldiers were ordered to deal with looters stealing from a bank, one of whom would become the ghost that haunts the speaker.
ex_zh: The word ‘looter’ stayed in his mouth like a stone, something he turned over and over to check if it still tasted like justification.

def_en: runs away quickly; flees on foot
def_zh: legs it up the road, looter legs it, suddenly legs it
quote_en: "And one of them legs it up the road, / probably armed, and possibly not." – One of the civilians runs away down the street, and the speaker is unsure whether he is carrying a weapon (Remains, 2008)
quote_zh: Armitage uses the colloquial ‘legs it’ to capture the soldier’s casual, demotic voice. The phrase is informal, almost slang, suggesting a situation that is being processed as routine rather than extraordinary. The present tense ‘legs’ gives the action an immediate, ongoing quality, and the /lɛɡz ɪt/ sounds are short and breathless, mimicking the sudden sprint. The word belongs to a semantic field of uncertain threat and rapid movement: ‘raiding’, ‘runs’, ‘open fire’. The crucial ambiguity follows immediately: ‘probably armed, and possibly not’. This uncertainty—which will echo through the poem in the soldier’s nightmares—is the moral wound that never heals. The phrase ‘legs it’ reduces the man to a moving target, a figure to be chased and shot. Armitage, through the colloquial register, subtly critiques the dehumanising language of military engagement.
ex_en: One of the looters suddenly legged it up the road, and the soldiers could not tell if he was carrying a weapon or running for his life.
ex_zh: He had legged it from the bank, from the bullets, from the country of the living, but in the soldier’s head he never stopped running, his legs still pumping through the doors of memory.

def_en: start shooting; discharge firearms at a target
def_zh: open fire on the looter, all three open fire, order to open fire
quote_en: "so all three of us open fire. / Three of a kind all letting fly, and I swear" – The speaker and his two comrades simultaneously shoot at the fleeing figure (Remains, 2008)
quote_zh: The phrase ‘open fire’ is a military euphemism that transforms killing into a technical, collective action. The word ‘open’ suggests a valve being released, an inevitability, while ‘fire’ distances the shooters from the bullets. The /oʊpən faɪər/ sounds are open-vowelled and almost calm, a moment of terrible clarity. The phrase belongs to a semantic field of coordinated violence: ‘letting fly’, ‘rounds’, ‘rips through’. The addition of ‘three of a kind’ emphasises the collective nature of the act, diffusing individual responsibility.
