Yet the speaker’s ‘I swear’ that follows breaks the clinical tone, as if he needs to testify to the truth of what he saw. Armitage captures the moment of irreversible action, when the casual decision to shoot becomes an eternal memory. The phrase is also the pivot from the poem’s opening, matter-of-fact narration into its psychological aftermath.
ex_en: All three soldiers simultaneously opened fire on the running man, their bullets striking him multiple times as he fled.
ex_zh: The order to open fire did not come from a superior; it came from somewhere inside all three of them, a dark, unanimous whisper that sounded exactly like fear.

def_en: with the inner surface turned outward; completely disordered; exposed in essence
def_zh: sort of inside out, turned inside out, inside out body
quote_en: "and he's there on the ground, sort of inside out," – The dead looter’s body is so damaged by the bullets that it appears to have been turned inside out (Remains, 2008)
quote_zh: Armitage’s colloquial ‘sort of inside out’ is horrifying in its vagueness. The phrase is a childlike approximation—‘sort of’—for something indescribable, a body so violated that its interior has become its exterior. The word ‘inside’ repeated in reverse (‘inside out’) mimics the physical inversion, the /ɪn/ and /aʊt/ sounds a chiasmus of destruction. It belongs to a semantic field of bodily ruin—‘rips through’, ‘pain itself’, ‘guts’, ‘torn apart’—that catalogues the physical fact of violent death. The phrase avoids medical precision in favour of the soldier’s shocked, unsanitised memory. Armitage, voicing a veteran’s testimony, shows that the mind cannot process such damage literally; it reaches for a child’s language to contain the uncontainable. The body is not just dead; it is wrong, a category violation.
ex_en: The looter lay dead on the ground, his body so ripped by the bullets that he appeared, in the soldier’s phrase, sort of inside out.
ex_zh: The memory had turned him inside out, so that the parts of himself he used to keep hidden—the guilt, the flashbacks, the sudden rages—were now what everyone else saw first.

def_en: throws something lightly or casually; here, a shocking gesture of casual care
def_zh: tosses his guts, tosses back, tosses into the body
quote_en: "One of my mates goes by / and tosses his guts back into his body." – A fellow soldier casually throws the dead man's spilled intestines back into the abdominal cavity (Remains, 2008)
quote_zh: Armitage uses ‘tosses’ to convey a gesture of terrible, mundane violence. The verb suggests a light, almost absent-minded movement, as one might toss a coin or a ball. Its present tense and colloquial register jar violently with its object: ‘his guts’. The /tɒsɪz/ sound is short and sharp, a flick of the hand. The word belongs to a semantic field of grotesque, routine horror: ‘sort of inside out’, ‘pain itself’, ‘carted off’. The casualness of ‘tosses’ is precisely the point: this is what soldiers do, they clean up, they carry on, they perform acts of appalling intimacy with a practised detachment. Armitage, who based the poem on interviews with veterans, shows how the mind protects itself by converting trauma into a series of mechanical actions. Yet the verb itself refuses to be neutral; it shocks the reader with its inappropriateness, mimicking the moral dissonance of war.
ex_en: One of his fellow soldiers walked past and casually tossed the dead man's spilled intestines back inside his body.
ex_zh: The gesture he remembered most was not the shooting but the toss—the way his friend had flung a man's insides back into the hollow of his belly as if returning a dropped scarf.

def_en: a dark stain left on a surface by blood; a persistent, haunting trace of violence
def_zh: blood-shadow stays, walk over the blood-shadow, shadow of blood
quote_en: "His blood-shadow stays on the street, and out on patrol / I walk right over it week after week." – The stain left by the dead man's blood remains on the road, and the speaker steps over it repeatedly during patrols (Remains, 2008)
quote_zh: Armitage coins the compound ‘blood-shadow’ to name a phenomenon both physical and psychological. A shadow is normally cast by a living body blocking light; this shadow is cast by the absence of a body, by the blood that remains after life has left. The word’s two stressed syllables, /blʌd/ and /ʃædoʊ/, are heavy and dark, one blunt, one fading. It belongs to a semantic field of haunting and persistence: ‘stays’, ‘walk right over it’, ‘week after week’, ‘bursts again’, ‘dug in’. The blood-shadow is a trace that cannot be erased, a stain that is also a ghost. The soldier walks over it, a literal crossing that becomes a ritual of repetition, an inescapable reminder. Armitage shows that the battlefield is not left behind; the street where the killing happened remains part of the soldier’s daily geography. The blood-shadow is the first hint that this story will not end with the body being ‘carted off’.
ex_en: The dead man's blood left a permanent shadow on the street, and every week the soldier walked directly over the stain.
ex_zh: The blood-shadow never faded; it just seeped deeper into the asphalt until it became a colour that only he could see, a private purple that marked the spot where his peace had bled out.

def_en: breaks open suddenly and violently; erupts with force
def_zh: bursts again, bursts through, memory bursts
quote_en: "But I blink / and he bursts again through the doors of the bank." – The speaker, now home on leave, finds that closing his eyes causes the dead man to erupt violently back into his consciousness (Remains, 2008)
quote_zh: Armitage uses ‘bursts’ to describe the involuntary return of the traumatic memory. The verb suggests an explosive, uncontrollable entry; the dead man does not glide or appear but bursts, as if kicking down the doors of the soldier’s mind. The /bɜːrsts/ sound is a cluster of hard consonants, a percussive eruption that mimics the violence it names. It belongs to a semantic field of inescapable recurrence: ‘blink’, ‘sleep’, ‘dream’, ‘bursts again’, ‘won’t flush him out’. The poem has moved from the physical street to the psychological interior, but the same verbs apply: the memory is as violent as the original bullets. The word ‘again’ is crucial; this is not a single nightmare but a loop, a night-after-night repetition. Armitage, informed by PTSD accounts, shows that the invasion of the memory is as real and as terrifying as the original encounter, and it respects no boundary between war zone and home.
ex_en: The moment the soldier blinked, the dead looter burst back through the doors of the bank in his mind, the scene replaying with horrible vividness.
ex_zh: Every time he closed his eyes, the past burst into the present like a gunshot through a window, and he was back on the road, the air full of dust and the smell of cordite.

def_en: to cleanse by a flow of water; to drive out or expose; to chase from hiding
def_zh: flush him out, drink and drugs won't flush, flush the memory
quote_en: "And the drink and the drugs won't flush him out –" – Neither alcohol nor narcotics can purge the dead man from the soldier's mind (Remains, 2008)
quote_zh: Armitage deploys ‘flush’ in a grimly ironic register. The verb belongs to the semantic field of hunting (flushing game from cover) and sanitation (flushing a toilet). The soldier tries both: he hunts the memory to expel it, and he tries to wash it away through intoxication. The /flʌʃ/ sound is harsh and liquid, a surge that fails. The word belongs to a semantic field of attempted purgation that the poem declares futile: ‘drink’, ‘drugs’, ‘flush’, ‘not left for dead’. The hyphen at the end of the line creates a pause, a moment of suspense before the inevitable negation. The memory is not waste to be disposed of; it is a presence that resists all chemical and psychological solvents. Armitage, in the tradition of Owen’s ‘mental cases’, shows that trauma is not a temporary ailment but a permanent inhabitant of the psyche. The flush fails, and the dead man remains.
ex_en: Neither alcohol nor drugs could flush the memory of the dead looter out of the soldier's head, no matter how hard he tried to drown it.
ex_zh: He tried to flush the memory away with whisky, but every empty bottle became a window through which the dead man stared back at him, still lying on the road, still inside out.

def_en: entrenched, especially in a military position; firmly established and hard to remove
def_zh: dug in behind enemy lines, dug in my head, well and truly dug in
quote_en: "he's here in my head when I close my eyes, / dug in behind enemy lines," – The dead man has become an entrenched presence in the soldier’s mind, a permanent, hostile occupant (Remains, 2008)
quote_zh: Armitage draws on the military terminology of trench warfare—‘dug in’—to describe the persistence of the traumatic memory. The phrase fuses the psychological and the tactical: the dead man is an enemy who has infiltrated the mind and fortified his position. The /dʌɡ ɪn/ sounds are hard and abrupt, a shovel hitting earth. It belongs to a semantic field of occupation and war transposed into the psyche: ‘enemy lines’, ‘patrol’, ‘sentries’, ‘bullets’. The metaphor suggests that the soldier’s mind is no longer his own sovereign territory; it has been invaded by a foreign presence that cannot be dislodged. The enjambment isolates ‘dug in’, making the phrase itself an entrenched fragment. Armitage echoes the language of earlier war poets—Owen’s ‘exposure’, Sassoon’s ‘haunted’—but applies it to a modern conflict and to an enemy who is not a nation but a single, killed man. The guerrilla war is now internal.
ex_en: The dead looter was dug in behind the enemy lines of the soldier's psyche, an entrenched presence that no therapy or time could remove.
ex_zh: The worst thing about being dug in, he thought, was that the enemy knew exactly where he was, and yet never attacked—just sat there, in the trench of his temporal lobe, waiting for him to look.

def_en: covered in or composed of blood; involving violence; a British intensifier, here literal and figurative
def_zh: bloody life, bloody hands, bloody memory
quote_en: "his bloody life in my bloody hands." – The poem concludes with the dead man’s life and the speaker’s hands, both described as stained with blood and guilt (Remains, 2008)
quote_zh: Armitage ends the poem on ‘bloody’, a word that holds two registers in unbearable tension. As an adjective, it means covered in blood, the literal gore of the killing. As a British colloquial intensifier, it captures the soldier’s inarticulate rage and guilt—a swear-word that conveys everything language cannot. The repetition—‘his bloody life in my bloody hands’—creates a mirroring that ties the dead and the living together eternally. The /blʌdi/ sound is blunt and fleshy, the double /d/ a dull thud. It belongs to a semantic field of violence and responsibility that has accumulated throughout the poem: ‘open fire’, ‘rips through’, ‘pain itself’, ‘guts’, ‘blood-shadow’. The final line refuses closure: the soldier is holding the life he took, and his hands will never be clean. Armitage, completing the narrative that began with a casual mission, arrives at a truth that no official debriefing can contain: the war has not ended, and the soldier is forever bound to the man he killed, in blood and in memory.
ex_en: The soldier holds the dead man's bloody life forever in his own bloody hands, the guilt and the violence inseparable from his own existence.
ex_zh: The blood on his hands had long since washed away, but the bloody word itself remained, an echo in the empty room of his conscience, repeating and repeating like a stuck record of a scream.
